titlestmt.titleproper: The Bert Hansen Collection of Medicine and Public Health in Popular Graphic Art
titlestmt.author: Katherine Isham

publicationstmt.publisher: Medical Historical Library, Harvey Cushing / John Hay Whitney Medical Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Medical Historical Library, Harvey Cushing / John Hay Whitney Medical Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday August 19, 2020 at 02:01

did.unittitle: The Bert Hansen collection of medicine and public health in popular graphic art

corpname.part: Medical Historical Library, Harvey Cushing / John Hay Whitney Medical Library

did.abstract: The collection comprises material collected by medical historian Bert Hansen to document depictions of medicine and public health presented to the general population of the United States from the mid-19th through early 21st centuries. Contents of the collection were produced between 1850 and 2010 and include complete and partial issues of periodical publications, prints, posters, film publicity materials, product brochures and promotional materials, calendars, and photocopies of related materials not included in the collection.

langmaterial.language: Materials in the collection are in English, German, and French.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Gift of Bert Hansen in multiple accessions between 2015 and 2017.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related materials may be found in Pam Coll 7 Comic book collection on medical themes and Pam Coll 8 William Helfand collection of medical ephemera held by the Medical Historical Library, Harvey Cushing / John Hay Whitney Medical Library, Yale University.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright status for collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged in four series: Series I. Early publications, 1850-1927. Series II. Later publications, 1920-2010. Series III. Ephemera, 1894-2008. Series IV. Reference copies, 1850-2007. The collection is further arranged by publication title, item title, and date. Much of the material is housed in archival folders of varying dimensions annotated by collection creator Bert Hansen. As a result, box and folder numbers are often non-sequential within series and sub-series.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Bert Hansen Collection of Medicine and Public Health in Popular Graphic Art. Historical Library, Harvey Cushing/John Hay Whitney Medical Library, Yale University.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Bert Hansen was born on February 5, 1944 in Chicago, Illinois. He received a bachelor's degree in chemistry from Columbia College in 1965 and a doctorate in the history of science from Princeton University in 1974. In 1978 he received a Mellon Fellowship at Harvard University and taught medical history courses there until 1979. Hansen held academic appointments at State University of New York at Binghamton; the University of Toronto; New York University; and Baruch College, City University of New York. His work focused on the natural sciences and magic in the middle ages; medical history in the nineteenth century, including the development of medical education, the work of Louis Pasteur, and diagnosing homosexuality; and popular culture perceptions and representations of medicine from the 19th through 21st centuries. Hansen's writings include numerous journal articles and book chapters and full-length publications Nicole Oresme and the Marvels of Nature: A Study of His De Causis Mirabilium and Picturing Medical Progress from Pasteur to Polio: A History of Mass Media Images and Popular Attitudes in America . Hansen is a prolific collector of publications, memorabilia, and artifacts related to his interests in medical history and mass media. In 2015, Hansen retired from Baruch College. He continues to write, collect, and present at academic conferences.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Bert Hansen Collection of Medicine and Public Health in Popular Graphic Art comprises material collected over a period of thirty years by medical historian Bert Hansen to document the visual representation of medical practice and research and public health in the United States. The contents of this collection are materials produced for the general public (not medical and public health professionals) from the mid-19th through the early 21st centuries. Hansen collected popular publications containing stories about medicine and medical practitioners or containing satirical illustrations using medical imagery, and ephemera with medical themes including prints, film publicity materials, posters, calendars, and brochures for medical devices. The bulk of materials in the collection were produced in the United States between 1850 and 2010. Series I. Early publications includes German and French materials, Series III. Ephemera includes French and Canadian materials, and Series IV. Reference copies may include copies of publications from other countries.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Bert Hansen uses a database to manage his extensive holdings and items in this collection are listed as they appeared in Hansen's database. As a result, items may consist of a single image, an article with multiple images, a complete issue of a publication, a single piece of ephemera, or multiple pieces of ephemera. Multiple items from one issue of a publication may be individually listed. Data and descriptions from Hansen's database have been edited to create item scope and content notes in this finding aid. These notes may include name of artist, description of images, author, historical context, details about printing processes, and related materials. Notes may also include Hansen's database identification numbers, which may point researchers to related materials in the collection.

did.unittitle: Early Publications

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged by title of publication and date. Materials are housed in folders as received from Hansen.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes partial and complete issues of numerous 19th and early 20th century periodical publications including Chic , Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , Harper's Monthly , Harper's Weekly , Life , McClure's , Judge , Puck , and Scientific American . Items in this series include articles and illustrations about medical developments, medical practitioners, and public health and satirical illustrations using medical imagery. Topics include Pasteur's treatments for rabies, cholera, diphtheria, polio, tuberculosis, vaccinations, hospitals, mental asylums, adulterated and unsafe foodstuffs, and public sanitation. Most images in the series are chromolithographs and wood engravings.

did.unittitle: "The President-Elect fruitlessly endeavors to escape from the Yum-Yum Office Seeker's, as well as from the Doctor Mary Walker Brigade. Enter Mrs. Garfield.--Tableau."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chic (1:10), page 16, back cover. Cartoon similar to early Puck and Judge. Image shows nice women in fancy hats and narrow waisted dresses protecting Garfield, standing up on a bureau, perhaps in a boarding house. A lady (in a long housecoat?) with broom and whisk broom stands in the open door apparently to remove a group of ugly women in pants, one of whom carries a sign reading "Gen. Mary Walker's Brigade of Office Seekers. Susan Anthony Lt. Col." Printed in pink, blue, and black ink. Hansen database #4393.

did.unittitle: "The American Colossus."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chic , page 8-9, centerfold. Color cartoon, similar to Puck and Judge. This image by Kendrick has a large bearded man straddling USA-Mexico border. He holds a string tied to a ship in the Nicaragua ship canal, and has railroad bond and mining shares in his pocket as well as "captain General pay" in his waistband. He smokes a cigar. Half his dress is a military uniform, the other half is Mexican style. This figure also appears in #4387. Hansen database #4386.

did.unittitle: "Young Democracy."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chic (1:18), page 42006, front cover, color. Very rare midwife image. "Young Democracy, Born at Cooper Institute, Tuesday, December 28, 1880. Monthly Nurse.--"Faix but yer a wake little crayture, all as wan as yer dead Mother. There's no sign av thrivin' about ye, at all, at all." In a windowless garret or basement, we see a dead donkey in bed to the left with this nurse holding the baby donkey, giving it a worried, cross-eyed look. Among the medications lying around: Develin plaster, Hancock powder, Bennett boodle, Hewitt's glue, Sun cholera mixture, Tilden Barl-y (sic) sugar. The nurse wears a checkered apron, a white bonnet or kerchief, stockings, and house slippers. Page 2 masthead is dated January 11, 1881, a day earlier than the cover date. "Published every Tuesday." No editorial on the cover, but there is a relevant 16-line verse about it, mentioning John Kelly, Irving, Tammany, and Hewitt. "Monthly nurse" was apparently a common term, often used in classified ads for women seeking positions. Seemingly this distinguished it from a children's nurse and graduate nurses were yet very uncommon. While not slovenly like Sairy Gamp, this figure carries no image of skill or professionalism. Hansen database #4397.

did.unittitle: "Coming Events Cast Their Shadows Before. Chic introduces her very juvenile and promising friend 1881 to the United States."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chic , page 8-9, centerfold. Color cartoon, similar to Puck and Judge. This is a new year's print with father time pushing an old 1880 away in front of a parade of figures led by a young 1881. Among the figures is the man in Mexican clothing from #4386, a man carrying an obelisk, a man carrying the Whitehouse (Garfield?), a man carrying a boat ("City of Rome, To Europe in Five Days"), and people carrying banners including Panama Canal, World's Fair, Hudson River Tunnel, St. Louis Census 600,000. In this image, the shadows are behind all the figures, not in front of them. Hansen database #4387.

did.unittitle: Daily Graphic (New York)
"The Spectre In Our Streets. (Respectfully Dedicated to the Street-Cleaning Bureau.)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (1:36), cover of complete issue. Shows skeleton and children playing in waste. Hansen database #2184.

did.unittitle: "Graphic Statues, No. 10--The Chairman at the Medico-Legal Dinner."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (1:40), cover of complete issue. Artist is Th. Wust. Hansen database #2185.

did.unittitle: "Mayor Havemeyer Disciplining the Aldermanic Monster."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (1:59), cover. Full-page drawing shows mayor, wielding a club and holding an edict of two committees, and creature with seven dog heads (Cerberus?). Tax Committee has John Wheeler, Geo. W. Andrews, and D. Moulton; Health Committee has Chas. F. Chandler, Dr. Stephen Smith, and Henry Claussen. Hansen database #59.

did.unittitle: "A Proper Reception For King Cholera."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (1:98), cover of a complete 8-page issue. Drawn by Wust. Mentions cities Nashville, Cincinnati, Pittsburgh, Washington, and Philadelphia. Wall behind men carries this text "Telegraphic Despatches. March of the Cholera. In Memphis, 59 deaths; Nashville 40, Cincinnati 10, Pittsburgh 5, Washington 3, Philadelphia 1." Hansen database #2186.

did.unittitle: "A Cartoon on the Half-Holiday Question."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (2:104), cover. Full-page drawing by Bellew. Manufacturer gives his race horses a rest, but not his factory workers. Bucolic scene, with small inset of several women and one child at work in a factory. Hansen database #60.

did.unittitle: "The Great Battle Between the Board of Health and the Marketmen."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York), page 196. Upper portion of page, shows battlefield with Board of Health vs. market men. Hansen database #61.

did.unittitle: "The Flood Catastrophe."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (4:374), cover of complete issue. Cover is picture of people and animals being swept away; story is about Mill River Valley flood caused by collapsed dam. Hansen database #4385.

did.unittitle: "Liberty Enlightening the World."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York), page 904. Bird's-eye view of the proposed statue showing great beams of light emitted by her crown, with harbor and land masses in the background. Note this is years before she is finished. Image is quarter-page or less, on a page mostly of display and classified ads. Hansen database #3158.

did.unittitle: "The Clinic."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (19:1858), page 56 of complete issue. Sketch of Eakins's painting of the Gross Clinic, one of a group of images from an art show. Captioned "The Clinic--Thomas Eakins." Story is "Pictures of the Day." Page 54 "Pictures of the Day" text reads "On pages 56 and 57 we produce copies of the most notable pictures in the second annual exhibition of the Society of American Artists, which opens today at the Kurtz Gallery (6 East 23rd St., New York City). These drawings and sketches will afford our numerous readers throughout the country an opportunity to make themselves familiar with the progress of art in America.…" No artists named in article. Hansen database #4010.

did.unittitle: "I Will Take Care of the Animals; Humanity Must Care for Itself."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (23:2293), front cover of complete issue. Vertically split image of Bergh crying over bullfight on the left, but on the right sternly evicting a poor working man (shoemaker) with wife and child from building owned by his Society. Artist not named, drawing signed only "C." Page 243 picture, news style, is of "the sham bull fight "of prior week. Editorial on page 240 makes clear that's what Bergh protested. Hansen database #3566.

did.unittitle: "The Paths of Pestilence, Fire and Murder in New York City."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (24:2463), page 841 in complete issue. Full page display of 3 maps of Manhattan showing (with black dots) the locations of small pox cases (prior 7 weeks!), fires (over $500 loss), and murders in 1880, with the text running between the maps. The front cover cartoon shows Uncle Sam politely refusing to help an Irishman and sending him instead to Mr. Gladstone. Hansen database #3017.

did.unittitle: "Cartoons on Current Topics: Walton's Woes."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (29:2891), front cover of complete issue. Cover has four "Cartoons on Current Topics" of which lower left is about burden of cleaning the streets, referring to Mr. Walton. Page 75 has illustration of Russian Jews in Hamburg Coming to America. Hansen database #62.

did.unittitle: "Abolish the Tax on Tobacco."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (30:3015), front cover. Implies problems will get worse if tax abolished. Includes pipe bowl as a skull; gravestone with "Smokers Take Warning"; man confined with straitjacket in asylum. Artist is Steele (first initial unclear, middle initial is probably O). Related editorial on page 248 in Pictures of the Day." Column 2 of page 248 contains one paragraph untitled article about a Philadelphia newspaper helping to catch 3 grave robbers. Hansen database #3327.

did.unittitle: "The Brutal Bigotry and Intolerance of Republicanism."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (34:3417), front cover. Full-page editorial cartoon by F. J. Willson, showing a boorish, fat Republican wearing a blindfold labeled "bigotry," dismissing General Sherman, whose wife is a Roman Catholic. "No! You may be a gallant soldier, you may possess culture and capacity, you may be honest --but your wife is a Roman Catholic!" An example of Willson's style and of the graphic presentation of prejudices in that era. The boorish guy resembles the caricatures of working-class Irish, usually associated with the Democratic party. Hansen database #4501

did.unittitle: "On the Wrong Side of the Herd."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (34:3469), front cover. Full-page editorial cartoon by Miranda, showing Chester A. Arthur being thrown off his horse in front of a herd of cattle labeled Blaine delegates. Hansen database #4500

did.unittitle: "The Opening of the New German Dispensary, No 137 Second Avenue, Last Saturday."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (35:3469), page 649. Exterior and interior images together on upper right quadrant of page. Hansen database #4444. .

did.unittitle: "The Hydrophobia Cure--The Experiments by M. Pasteur."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (34:3485), page 803. Three-part illustration on the upper half of page, with a column of unrelated text on either side. The main caption includes "from L'Illustration." Illustrations are "Cage for inoculated dogs," "Effect of inoculation" showing a monkey in a cage, and "M. Pasteur in his Laboratory" showing Pasteur between two rows of rabbit cages. Hansen database #4398.

did.unittitle: "Appropriate to the Season."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (34:3493), front cover. Full-page editorial cartoon by Miranda, showing well-off families leaving on vacation, luggage labeled Saratoga, Newport, and Europe, with a young girl giving money to a nun collecting for the poor. An example of Miranda's style and of sympathetic presentation of religious charity. This era has plenty of anti-Catholic cartoons, often mocking donations to the church as going for corruption or terrorism. Since the Daily Graphic cover of March 27, 1884, by F. J. Willson (here #4501) is critical of anti-Catholic bias, it suggests that Daily Graphic might have been particularly sensitive on this issue and sympathetic to Roman Catholicism. Hansen database #4498

did.unittitle: "Castle Garden as a Matrimonial Market."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily Graphic (New York) (34:3494), front cover. Full-page editorial cartoon by Miranda, showing male-female couples deliberating or negotiating, neutrally shown, without tension among the characters. An example of Miranda's style and of sympathetic presentation of new immigrants. Hansen database #4499

did.unittitle: Swill milk

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 377: Three stacked scenes. Exposure of milk trade: cows in yard and stalls. Hansen database #1.

did.unittitle: Swill milk

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 404: Three stacked scenes: feed mill on top, cows with inspectors at bottom. Hansen database #2.

did.unittitle: "City Enormities--Every Brute Can Beat His Beast."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (21:526), cover, page 81. Engraving signed BGHS (=Berghaus?). Relevant to Bergh's founding of ASPCA; Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper later reprinted this proudly. Hansen database #3.

did.unittitle: "The Board of Health in Session--Receiving Reports of the Inspectors at Police Headquarters, Mulberry Street, New York."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (25:550), full front cover, page 49. Drawing of Board of Health in session, bghs = Berghaus? Hansen database #4.

did.unittitle: "The Cattle Plague: Examination of Diseased Cattle by the Members of the N. Y. Board of Health, Gov. Ward, of New Jersey, and Veterinary Surgeons, at the Abattoirs, near Bergen, N. J."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 377, full page engraving. Additional text on page 375. Hansen database #5.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Medical College for Women. East Twelfth Street and Second Avenue--Student dissecting a Leg."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:759), page 65 in complete issue. Cover of woman dissecting corpse, plus article and 3 more illustrations. Hansen database #6.

did.unittitle: "Women Doctors."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:759), pages 66-67. An article essay with four images of women's medical college (dissection etc.). In this op-ed like piece by A. K. Gardner, M.D., he confesses that he made unkind and unfair judgments about them twenty years earlier, and says he was wrong. He has fairly strong praise for them now (if sexist in its assumptions). Hansen database #3994.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Medical College for Women. East Twelfth Street and Second Avenue--The Anatomical Lecture Room."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:759), page 72 in complete issue. Upper image of two. Hansen database #2432.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Medical College for Women. East Twelfth Street and Second Avenue--The General Lecture Room."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:759), page 72 in complete issue. Lower image of two. Hansen database #2433.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Medical College for Women. East Twelfth Street and Second Avenue--Dissecting Room--Students at Work."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:759), page 73 in complete issue. This the final image of three. Hansen database #2434.

did.unittitle: "New York City--A real blessing to the poor--the new north and east river public baths."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 296. Half-page engraving on the new public baths. Hansen database #2398.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Interior View of the Hospital for Sunstruck Patients, Centre Street, City Hall Park."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (:968), page 348. Half page engraving. Hansen database #2400.

did.unittitle: "The Smallpox Excitement."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (34:862), cover of a complete issue. Three images on smallpox: "The Small Pox Excitement-Strapping the calf to the operating table," "The Small Pox Excitement-Dr. Chambon inoculating the calf with vaccine virus," "The Small Pox Excitement-Dr. Chambon vaccinating patients in his parlor with virus taken directly from the animal. See page 54." Hansen database #2402.

did.unittitle: "Rising Above Party."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (34:867), front cover. Matt Morgan caricature of Carl Schurz with two others (is one Greeley?). Hansen database #7.

did.unittitle: "The dead, the dying and the crippled in the credit mobilier ward of the Union Pacific Hospital."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 368. J. Keppler. Hansen database #1959.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Commencement Exercises of the Women's Medical College at Steinway Hall."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (36:915), page 73. "Dr. S. Willetts Presenting Diplomas to the Graduates." Hansen database #2403.

did.unittitle: "The Cultivation of Drug Clerks."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (36:936), page 406 in a complete issue. Article about drug store clerks lack of pharmaceutical knowledge. Also pages 416-417 story about Shakers in Lebanon, New York with five pictures. Additional six pictures of Shakers in September 13, 1873 issue. Hansen database #2571.

did.unittitle: "New York City--A Scene In Bellevue Hospital--Ladies Visiting the Sick."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (37:937), page 9 in a complete issue. Full page image, one side of centerfold. Text on page 5. Also, pages 12-13 have six pictures of Shakers working and dining. September 6, 1873 issue has five pictures of Shakers. Hansen database #2404.

did.unittitle: "A summer Scene in NYC: A Persecuted Dog on a Leading Avenue."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (30:978), front cover. Hand-colored, policeman shooting at a dog. Hansen database #8.

did.unittitle: "A Scene In The New York Eye and Ear Infirmary."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (40:1019), page 73. "Second Avenue and Thirteenth Street, During The Hours For The Reception Of Patients." A short article appeared on page 72. Hansen database #2410.

did.unittitle: "Played Out; or, Return of the Strolling Players from Connecticut."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (40:1021), front cover. By J. Keppler, Uncle Sam greeting Blaine and other corrupt Republicans, fashioned as a troupe of players. Includes two KKK icons. Hansen database #9.

did.unittitle: "The Democratic Doctors Attempting to Vaccinate Political Tramps in Congress."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (48:129), page 112. Hand tinted. Hansen database #10.

did.unittitle: "New York City. Sanitary Precaution against a smallpox epidemic. An Inspector of the Board of Health Vaccinating Tramps in a Station-House."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (48:129), page 112. Half page engraving, image covers lower half page, with a short text above. Hansen database #3218.

did.unittitle: "How the Street-Cleaning Authorities in New York Guard Against Summer Epidemics."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 372. Wide cartoon, upper page, by Opper. Worker for Street Cleaning Department tells Father Knickerbocker that you can't do a mile a day very well. Hansen database #11.

did.unittitle: "New York City--The Agitation for Clean Streets--The Hearing of the Police Commissioners Before Mayor Grace, on the Charges Preferred by Him."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 148. Full-page scene, anonymous, agitations for clean streets, hearing of police commissioner before Mayor Grace. Hansen database #12.

did.unittitle: "New York City--Health Officers Vaccinating Russian and Polish Emigrants on Board the Steamship 'Victoria' at Quarantine, April 25th."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (52:1337), front cover of complete issue. Angry lady shaking her fist. Hansen database #13.

did.unittitle: "Compulsory Vaccination in New Jersey--A Street Scene During the Smallpox Scare."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper , page 200-201. Image is quarter of double-page spread, New Jersey Compulsory Vaccination in the street with crowds around doctors. Additional text page 199. Hansen database #15.

did.unittitle: "M. Pasteur, the French chemist and biologist, who recently celebrated his seventieth birthday."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (76:1948), page 29. Engraving is center of Edelfelt painting, which isn't credited on this page. Relevant text on page 19. Hansen database #24.

did.unittitle: "Quarantine Precautions Against Epidemic Diseases--Examination of Steerage Passengers Arriving at New York from Foreign Ports."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (56:1456), front cover. Health officer or doctor examining steerage woman. Hansen database #16.

did.unittitle: "Bogus Butter."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (58:1488), cover of complete issue. Cover story expose of oleomargarine, text continued on page 91. Cover image captioned "Bogus butter-making. An oleomargarine wagon making its rounds among the slaughter-houses, collecting the materials of which the bogus compound is made." Full page of pictures also on page 89. Hansen database #17.

did.unittitle: "Bogus Butter-making.--The Process of manufacture--A Fat-Collecting Wagon, With Its Unsavory Load, On Its Way to the Factory."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (58:1488), page 89 in complete issue. Half-page image from cover story "Bogus Butter," an expose of oleomargarine. Text continued on page 91. Hansen database #406.

did.unittitle: "Separating the Fat From the Entrails."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (58:1488), page 89, in complete issue. Half-page image from cover story "Bogus Butter," an expose of oleomargarine. Text continued on page 91. Hansen database #405.

did.unittitle: "The recent torrid weather--visit of the Disinfecting Corps of the New York Board Of Health to an underground lodging-house in the Five Points."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (59:1513), cover. There is a text on page 70. Hansen database #2430.

did.unittitle: "Virus Farm of the Board of Health."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (59:1521), page 205. Three images: "Extracting The Virus," "The Elevator," "The Stable," together called "New York City --The virus farm of the Board of Health no. 302 Mott St." Short text above the image is titled "The Vaccine Bureau." Hansen database #2399.

did.unittitle: "The French Chemist, M. Pasteur, Experimenting On A Chloroformed Rabbit."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (61:1578) page 300. Lower half of page includes four portraits of Newark boys. Hansen database #1397.

did.unittitle: "Canada--The Recent Smallpox Epidemic In Montreal--Vaccinating American-Bound Passengers On A Train of the Grand Trunk Railway."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (61:1579), page 316. Hansen database #1398.

did.unittitle: "New York City--The Hospital Ambulance Service--An Accident to a Factory Hand."

did.unittitle: "Foully Murdered."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Harper's Weekly (21:1071), full front cover. Nast, shows Justice in New York as a female corpse on a slab in the morgue with windows allowing for public's view. The smiling crowd is labeled Tammany Ring Hall. Slab is like a table except for the faucet at the slightly higher head end. Her sword and scales are on a clothes hook on the wall, probably as a deceased's personal effects were exhibited to help identification. Page 518 editorial, "Tweed and the Attorney General" seems to be the matter referenced in the cover cartoon. Hansen database #96.

did.unittitle: "A Board of Health Doctor in a New York Tenement"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Harper's Weekly (33:1703), page 637, cover. By W. A. Rogers. Although this is a "tenement" there is an orientalist print (=Sephardic?) on the wall above a fireplace mantle; also note the doctor is taking pulse from wrist, without using a watch, while listening directly to the chest (heart or lungs?) without a stethoscope of any kind. No medical accoutrements other than the doctor's bag are seen. On page 651 (not included), the cover picture is mentioned: "The picture which appears on another page is an accurate representation of what was seen on a recent trip made in company with Dr. Davies Coxe, of the Summer Corps of the Board of Health." The "summer corps" is discussed in an editorial in Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper (54:1401), July 29, 1882, page 354 without illustrations. Hansen database #1540.

did.unittitle: "The Syms Operating Theatre of the Roosevelt Hospital, New York. "

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Harper's Weekly , (37:1888), page 187-188. Five photographs on page 188, text on page 187 in columns 1-2, "The Syms Operating Theatre." A $350,000 bequest on which Dr. McBurney advised the decedent. Nothing in article about breakthroughs or large-scale change. A mention of septic cases, distinguishing them apparently from others less likely to soil the surgical instruments. Praise for design so all can see without crowding and leaning over. All of page 188 is photographs, one large and four small of the building and the interior. Largely clean, empty rooms; no significant activity. Real photographs, not the grey watercolor-like images based on them, but still without strong blacks and whites for adequate contrast. Hansen database #134.

scopecontent.p: Harper's Weekly (38:1933), page 9. Four vignettes in water-color style, with Dr. Pardee in one of them. Relevant text is on page 10 in columns 2-3: "The Virus Farm of the New York Board of Health." This is about smallpox; no hint of diphtheria work yet; no mention of Pasteur or Gibier. Image captions are: 1. The "Farm," 2. "Dr. Pardee inoculating a healthy Heifer," 3. "Gathering the 'Cropage'," and 4. "Vaccinating Applicants at the 'Farm'." Hansen database #1648.

did.unittitle: "Questions of the Day."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Upper left is Bergh vivisecting the vivisectors; lower left is a girl punished by Judge for wearing boys' clothing to get better work. Page 2 had some text in column called "Questions of the Day." The final paragraph reads: "The Judge believes it fair play for everyone and proposes that even Henry Bergh shall have a chance. The vivisectors who have trifled with his feelings, and who have apparently resolved themselves into a mob to drive him from pillar to post should also have a chance. Then let Mr. Bergh use his scalpel upon them, just for a change, and we venture the prediction that he will be encouraged by the shouts of the people, `Let the good work go on'." did.unittitle: "His First Batch Of Patients."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (15:387), page 376-377, center. By Hamilton, it's B. Harrison, M. D., U. S. Physician. Hansen database #955.

did.unittitle: "Hopeless Cases. Doctor Brown-Sequard Randall. Take Them Away. They're Too Dead for Treatment."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (16:411), page 336-337. By Grant Hamilton, note the masthead is Judge, not The Judge. On the left side, Dr. Brown-Sequard Randall sits in an upholstered green wing chair, holding a bottle of fluid in left hand and a large syringe with hollow needle in his right. The needle is curved at the end, with its hole on the side just where the curve starts, the syringe is a simple piston. The Brown-Séquard figure is Samuel Jackson Randall, a Democratic congressman from Pennsylvania, who had lost President Cleveland's favor two years earlier. He had been a protectionist since the 1860s despite that position's being unpopular in his party. He is now well supplied with two large tanks of "Protectionism" as an "Elixir of Life." But it's too late, he says, for this rejuvenation juice to revive the Democratic Party and its debilitated Free Trade policy. The central figures are Charles A. Dana (with a tag to identify him as owner-editor of the New York Sun) and Joseph Pulitzer (proprietor of the New York World, apparently recognizable without a label), who have brought a tiger (the Democratic Party, see tag on the tail) in for Doctor Randall's protectionism treatment. At the right, "Free Trade" is being carried in by two men coming from the U.S. Capitol, visible behind them in the doorway: Roger Quarles Mills, Congressman (later Senator) from Texas, and Henry Watterson, editor of the Louisville Courier-Journal. Watterson, not "Waterson" (Is the mis-spelling a further joke on this fellow editor?) was an aggressive advocate of free-trade ideas. By chance, Randall died at age 62 in April 1890, less than a year after this cartoon appeared. Sequard appears several times in caption and on the inside, but never with an accent. June (and early July?) issues seem to have several verbal quips about doctors mistakenly thinking a man dead and wanting to bury him. Hansen database #2049.

did.unittitle: "An Unwelcome Visitor"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (17:431), page 235, front cover. Hamilton, "La Grippe--'Good-bye; I've had a splendid reception. Uncle Sam-- 'Don't come again'." Bearded man with cloak and Russian-style fur hat wears ribbon labelled "Russian La Grippe" and carries a suitcase labelled influenza. Uncle Sam has ready at hand: pills, plaster, medicines, but no thermometer. Hansen database #37.

did.unittitle: "Left! The poor little wretch has no friends now."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (19:486), page 311, front cover, in bound volume. By Hamilton, "Left! The poor little wretch has no friends now." A baby left in snow outside the New York Sun Political Foundling Asylum. The note with it is signed by Hill. The baby is Hill's presidential boom. Dana looks out the window of the asylum. Hansen database #2075.

did.unittitle: "Where the Blame Lies."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (19:494), page 458-459, center, in bound volume. By Hamilton. "Judge to Uncle Sam--'If immigration was (!) properly restructured, you would not be troubled with Anarchy, Socialism, the Mafia, and such kindred evils'." Hansen database #2076.

did.unittitle: "A Picture of Patience."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:495), page 1, front cover, in bound volume. By Victor, a pig, crying, dressed as Uncle Sam, sitting outside the door of Germany, which has a sign "No Admittance for the American Hog." France's gate says "The American Pig Excluded." Issue has no text on this; all content is jokes and very short pieces. Hansen database #2077.

did.unittitle: "Too pleasant to be true."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:496), page 24-25, center, in bound volume. By Victor, "Judge dreams that King Humbert recalls all his objectionable countrymen now sojourning in the United States. Alas! It is only a dream." Hansen database #2078.

did.unittitle: "Spring Gardening. The rival gardeners rushing the season."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:496), page 32, back cover, in bound volume. By Hamilton. Hansen database #2079.

did.unittitle: "A mighty poor exchange. From the sublime to the ridiculous."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:497), page 33, front cover, in bound volume. By Victor. Hansen database #2080.

did.unittitle: "Spring Farming."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:500), page 90-91, center, in bound volume. Unsigned. "The Political Farmers are already hard at work repairing, fixing up things and making ready for anticipated large autumn crops." i.e. trying to fix up the Democratic Old Homestead. Includes Dana tacking up a sign: "Sun Mixture: Sure Cure for Cleveland Colic." Hansen database #2081.

did.unittitle: "A Social Problem."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:501), page 11, back cover, in bound volume. By Hamilton, two scenes attacking the Society for the Prevention of Vice. Upper and lower captions: "Why is it that our daily newspapers are allowed to retail as `news' the most abominable indecencies for our children to read--While the art dealer is arrested for selling classics and works of art which only the most cultured can appreciate or understand?" Issue has no relevant text. Hansen database #2082.

did.unittitle: "The strong man of the Republican Party."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:502), front cover, in bound volume. By Gillam. Hansen database #2083.

did.unittitle: "Sporting Mania."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (20:506), page 183, front cover, in bound volume. Victor, "Sporting, sporting everywhere, and nothing else to read." Man in the cartoon is wearing around his neck either a watch or a magnifier that resembles the 1860 Lincoln "stethoscope." Hansen database #2084.

did.unittitle: "He is getting very, very sick!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:518), front cover, in bound volume. Shows boy getting sick from smoking tobacco in pipe, political cartoon, not anti-smoking. Hansen database #2085.

did.unittitle: "The Age of Electricity."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:518), page 398, back cover, in bound volume. By Hamilton, nine inventions, e.g. electric cartoon machine, battery powered organ (like organ grinder), electric club for policeman. Hansen database #2086.

did.unittitle: "When the Rain-Machine Comes Into General Use."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:522), page 464, back cover, in bound volume. Hamilton, six vignettes. Hansen database #2087.

did.unittitle: "The Age of Invention."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:524), page 496, back cover, in bound volume. Hamilton, "Future Businessman (to Miss Nature)--'I have no more use for you; modern inventions have taken your place. I press the button, they do the rest'." He has buttons marked Heat Machine, Snow Machine, Light Machine, Ice Machine, Rain Machine; also visible are Butter Machine and an incubator with hatching chicks. Hansen database #2088.

did.unittitle: "Thanksgiving Judge."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:528), page 558, in bound volume. Short anecdotes and jokes, some with medical theme. Hansen database #2089.

did.unittitle: "Judge Christmas."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:529), page 25, in bound volume. Cartoon shows "ill treated" tramp tricking people into thinking the sausage contains dog meat. Hansen database #2090.

did.unittitle: "The bibulous Snake-Charmer's Mistake."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge ( 21:530), page 617, in bound volume. Cartoon by F. M. Howarth shows snake charmer suffering ill effects of drinking and running to a temperance mission. Hansen database #2091.

did.unittitle: "Tweed's Protege and Successor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:531), page 635, front cover. Reverse has series of verbal jokes about health and medicine. Hansen database #51.

did.unittitle: "The week before Christmas."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:531), page 650, back cover, in bound volume. Includes Keeley's Gold Cure in one vignette. Hansen database #2092.

did.unittitle: "Some Valuable 'Spaces' for Advertisers."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (21:532), back cover, in bound volume. Cartoon depicts advertising on related parts of people, includes "Dr. No's Catarrh Cure" on a man's nose, "Terrick's Corn Plasters" on a foot, "Dr. Slick's Patent Chest Protector" on a man's chest. Hansen database #2093.

did.unittitle: Full year in bound volume.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (19:481 - 21:532), full year in one bound volume. Hansen database #148.

did.unittitle: "Quarantined."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (23:572), page 228. By Hamilton, Uncle Sam as "U.S. Health Officer (on White House dock; The Judge is there too.): `No, gentlemen, you cannot land here; you have a bad attack of the free-trade plague, and it will take several years to fumigate your properly!'" Hansen database #1954.

did.unittitle: "A Prophetic Picture--The World's Fair Will Be Closed by the Cholera."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (24:586), page 8-9, center. By Victor, "The cholera has broken out in Hamburg--an emigrant ship lately landed over twenty-two hundred emigrants in New York." Gate at right says "Closed on Account of Cholera Brought through Indiscriminate Immigration." A specter labeled "cholera" in front of gate stops Uncle Sam and "Miss Columbia," both in travelling clothes (i.e. they are tourists); behind them is the lake and buildings of the Chicago Fair (i.e. this is not the ocean, the location is not Hamburg or New York). There is no text about this image in the magazine. Hansen database #2116.

did.unittitle: "The Advertising Craze. It is enough to make a nervous man think he has them all."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (25:636), page 436, back cover. Unsigned, man and woman confronted by medicinal ads everywhere. Hansen database #52.

did.unittitle: "Sugar-Coated. Dr. Grover 'I've made that pill nice and pleasant for you. It's heavily coated with sugar'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (27:671), page 113, front cover. Gillam, Sam holds tariff pill from Wilson Druggist. Hansen database #41.

did.unittitle: "An Effort to Resuscitate a Bad Case."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (29:733), page 284-285, center. By Gillam, a drowned Tammany tiger from a wrecked ship is laying over a barrel labelled "The Rum Question." A paper on the ground says "Drowned in the tidal wave of 1894." Dana from the New York Sun tries to give the tiger a spoon of "N.Y. Sun Cholera Mixture" while politicians and publishers give advice. Hansen database #42.

did.unittitle: "The President's Latest Photograph."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (30:749), page 117, cover. Hamilton, "Note--Judge applies Dr. Roentgen's wonderful discovery of photographing the interior of the most opaque bodies, with surprising results." Shows wheels turning in his head named Blunder Wheel, Free Trade Folly, 3rd Term, also thoughts of Blue Fish, etc. Hansen database #2144.

did.unittitle: "Racing Season of '96. Can they get a race out of the old nag?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (30:759), cover. Hamilton, Democrats trying to get Free-Trade donkey ready to race a Protection horse led out by the Judge. Pills and bottles and plaster. Also Olney holds a large syringe or small clyster labelled "Jingo." A Weak free-trade donkey versus an upright protection race horse led by Judge, as the groom. The horse medications resemble human ones: "Silver Pills" "Jingo Syringe" "Carlisle Plaster" and bottles of "Income Tax" and "Wilson Mixture." Hansen database #43.

did.unittitle: "Only a Comet. Uncle Sam 'It will soon be out of sight'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (31:785), page 273, cover of complete issue. Hamilton, nicely detailed depiction. Hansen database #47.

did.unittitle: "The Campaign Liar."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (31:785), page 280-281, center in complete issue. The price of wheat is going up every day, while the price of silver is decreasing, contrary to Mr. Bryan's arguments." Hansen database #48.

did.unittitle: "Funny Foot-Ball Folks."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (31:785), page 288, back cover of complete issue. Hansen database #49.

did.unittitle: "Canned Beef."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (36:912), back. Two by Zim, joke about canned beef in the news and canned dog, i.e. dog running with tin cans tied to its tail. Hansen database #50.

did.unittitle: "A Tough Job. Can Doctor Lorenz Cleveland straighten out the old Democratic mule?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (44:1109), cover. Grant Hamilton, in a surgical theater, empty except for a smiling Sam, a white-gowned Cleveland rolls up his sleeves. At hand are two tanks: "Political Oxygen" and "Cleveland's Ether." Labels on mule's leg are "Expansion Leg," "Financial Leg," and "Tariff Leg." Page 2 has editorial of one very long paragraph: ". . . . The methods of Dr. Lorenz, the eminent Vienna surgeon, as known to all and will probably at no distant period be practiced by specialists throughout the land…." This might be the earliest white-coated doctor. Hansen database #948.

did.unittitle: "A Sardonic Soliloquy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (44:1109), page 16, back cover. Medical content in Irish saloon cartoon. 1 "Hungry Hank-I'm sent here by de doctor of de small-pox pest-house ter get-" 2 Hank (pouring it down) "Oh, Hank! Hank! I'm afraid dat dat inventive head o'yours will make a rich millionare of you yet." Hansen database #952.

did.unittitle: "In The Hopeless Ward. Dr. Osler, the scientist, has discovered that man, after sixty years of age, is useless. The Republican doctors have a patient one hundred and twenty-five years old who never was of any use."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1223), centerfold. Dalrymaple, patient is old man Democratic Party. Remedy bottles all around. Theodore Roosevelt, as "Dr. Roosevelt" holds "Republican Chloroform." Second man has large syringe labelled "Dr. Cortelyou" and a hooked knife labelled in red ink, "P. M. Gen." Very long paragraph on page 2 opens, "Dr. Osler recently opened his mouth and found himself famous. An unconscious humorist, his name instantly became a household word; for it is not exaggerating the fact to say that by the utterance of a few phrases he became more widely known in a few hours than in the many years of unflagging effort devoted to the cause of education. It is now believed by many people that the object of his mission aboard is not to become a fellow at Oxford, but to accept a lucrative position on the staff of Punch...." He could decide when a joke is old and should be chloroformed. Hansen database #434.

did.unittitle: "The Death-Dealer."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1223), cover. Calls Cigarette Trust "The Death Dealer." Hansen database #946.

did.unittitle: "He Contracted For Anything."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1223), back cover. Two by Zim; lower one is "He Contracted for Anything." Pun about contracting a disease. Mrs. Casey (giving baby a spoon of medicine)--Doctor says he "contracted" measles, whooping cough, and mumps all at once. Mr. Casey--Takes after Grandfather Dolin, the biggest contractor in Tammany hall. Hansen database #947.

did.unittitle: "Uncle Sam's Sanitary Barber-Shop. NEXT!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1230), center, in complete issue. T. R., lots of references to germs, germ-ine, microbe-ine, disinfectants, bichloride, etc. Hansen database #435.

did.unittitle: "The New American Eagle."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1230), cover of complete issue. Hansen database #944.

did.unittitle: "A Spring-day Wish Of The Class" and "No Cause For Alarm."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (48:1230), back of complete issue. Two images. Hansen database #945.

did.unittitle: "An Error in Diagnosis."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (68:1735), single sheet. Color cartoon signed by "L. Fellows," illustrates a humerous short story by J. A. Waldron. Shows elegant lady patient in bed with bon bons and rubber hot water bottle, and seated doctor holding a thermometer. Hansen database #2902

did.unittitle: "Ladies Home Journal."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Judge (93:2394), Satire of Ladies Home Journal by Judge. "Beauty hints for the business woman." Hansen database #3104.

did.unittitle: "Bergh's Bastinado: A Hint from the Apostle of Humanity."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (1:22), page 8-9, center. By Keppler. Bergh, wearing a fez and holding a whip, has dogs lounging on a sofa, while editors/publishers and politicians have their legs held in stocks for whipping the soles of their feet. Hansen database #231.

did.unittitle: "Riding His Hobby."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (2:30), page 16, back cover. Four vignettes. Mocks Herald for involvement with Stanley's adventures in Africa. "Wanted: More Discoverers." Newspaper sensationalism. Use of single-color overlay, and poorly registered too. Hansen database #292.

did.unittitle: "Vanderbiltiana."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (2:37), page 16, back cover. J. Keppler, nine numbered vignettes, including: #4 "He acquiesced in the idea of putting his Ma in a lunatic asylum." #7 "Till the 'old man' died, when he skipped of with $95,000,000." #9 "Cornelius John [Vanderbilt] trying to pull down the young American Centaur." Faint single-color overlay. Hansen database #293.

did.unittitle: "The Streets of New York."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (2:39), page 16, back cover. Seven vignettes, #7 shows dame New York sweeping out the street cleaning bureau; #6 New York City gets Paris prize of 1878 for dirtiest city in the world; #2 ashes and garbage; #8 refers to typhoid fever; #5 to hogs eating in the streets; #4 has woman asking policeman to find her little boy lost in the deep muck, title is "A Worse Fate than Charlie Ross's (reference to first major kidnapping for ransom). Single-color overlay, and poorly registered too. Hansen database #294.

did.unittitle: "Let Us All Kiss and Be Friends."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (2:40), page 16, back cover. J. Keppler. "Puck hopes to see Minister Welsh's sweet example followed as above." Eight vignettes. In the center, J. Welsh, minister to court of St. James (i.e. Britain) is kissing a man (who?) at the head of a long line. Pairs include Beecher and Tilton, Grant and Dana, Conkling and Hayes, Puck and MacMahon. Use of faint single-color overlay. Hansen database #295.

did.unittitle: "How to Prevent 'Awful Catastrophes.' The Directors should be made to travel over their own roads, as above."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (2:46), front cover. J. Keppler, four men are on the cow-catcher as railroad train passes over a bridge. Only color overlay seems to be grey. Additional text page 2. Hansen database #149.

did.unittitle: "Diogenes-Puck Discovers an Honest Lawyer."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (4:102), front cover of complete issue. Hansen database #150.

did.unittitle: "What We Eat and Drink."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (4:102), page 8-9, center in complete issue. "Eleventh Commandment: Thou Shalt Not commit Adulteration." Nine vignettes include swill milk, chemicals, poisoned candy. Hansen database #232.

did.unittitle: "New York's Carnival Fantasia--The Last Fortnight Before Lent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (4:102), back cover of complete issue. Hansen database #296.

did.unittitle: "The Murderous Policy of the 'L' Roads."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:133), page 464, back cover. JK (or TK?). Mr. Field (owner?) says he's not using the L, "since we reduced the men's pay, it isn't very safe, I think." High dividends and low wages. Shows a signal man exhausted at such wages. Main scene is collision between two trains labelled low wages and the private directors (with high dividends). Hansen database #297.

did.unittitle: "Is There No Bergh for This Unfortunate Beast?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:135), page 483, front cover. Keppler, New York City as a horse burdened by city administration, debt, over-taxation, and corruption, being pulled by Kelly(?) and another man, with two others holding it back. Background might be City Hall. One color overlay, so nicely done it seems like more. Hansen database #151.

did.unittitle: "The Friend of the Brutes."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:138), page 552, back cover. J. A. Wales, apparently sympathetic? Bergh escorts two officers, Fleming as a dog and Williams as a cat or monkey, into the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals. Hum. Bergh, Pres(iden)t. Hansen database #298.

did.unittitle: "Do Not Fear, We'll Protect You!"

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:141), front cover of complete issue. J. Keppler, constitution shielding public schools. Hansen database #152.

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:141), page 596-597, center in complete issue. Keppler. Hansen database #233.

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:141), back cover of complete issue. J. A. Wales. Hansen database #299.

did.unittitle: "The Streets of New York--Running the Gauntlet of Horrors."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:142), page 624, back cover. Early two-color printing, peach and light blue. J. A. Wales. Nice couple is surrounded by beggars. Puck to the Commission of c & c (charities and corrections?): "If you can't remove these people from the streets on the score of Charity, do it for Decency's sake." Background shows homes for the poor, the blind, the aged, and cripples. One is named Mt. Sinai. Hansen database #300.

did.unittitle: "You May Bark! But Don't Dare to Bite!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:143), front cover of complete issue. J. Keppler, Uncle Sam as watch dog protecting public school funds from parochial schools. Hansen database #153.

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:143), page 632-633, center in complete issue. Unsigned, puts contemporaries in zodiac and other constellations. Hansen database #234.

did.unittitle: "Scientific Testimony--Showing how it Acts On the Average Juryman."

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:143), back cover, J. A. Wales. Hansen database #301.

did.unittitle: "May They Fulfill Their Promises--The New Year and the New Light!"

scopecontent.p: Puck (6:147), page 702-703, center. By J. Keppler. New year is seated on Edison light bulb labeled "A New Light to the World." Father Time is carrying out candles and kerosene lamps. Hansen database #235.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (6:148), back cover, J. A. Wales, weak mortar for crowded tenements under construction. Copy 2. Hansen database #303.

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:162), page 87, front cover of complete issue. J. Keppler, pope stealing charity from the poor Irish immigrant. Hansen database #154.

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:162), page 94-95, center. By J. A. Wales, shows a diploma mill. Copy 1. Hansen database #236.

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:162), page 94-95, center in complete issue. By J. A. Wales, shows a diploma mill. Copy 2. Hansen database #237.

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:162), back cover of complete issue. J. Keppler, Tilden and another politician using axes of Fraud and Grantism to cut down the Liberty Tree which is being eaten by caterpillars of frauds, bribery, nepotism, demogogism, and cent(r)alization. Hansen database #304.

did.unittitle: "The Great Congressional Oleomargarine Investigation."

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:163), page 109. Black and white full-page cartoon by F. Opper with five scenes. Hansen database #397.

did.unittitle: "Mr. Tilden's Physical Condition. From a Democratic and from a Republican Point of View."

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:163), back cover, page 122. F. Opper, "Democratic soft-soaper" and Republican "slang-slinger." Hansen database #305.

did.unittitle: "A Moving Day Fancy."

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:164), page 142, back cover. F. Opper, with Edison lamp and many others including Bergh, Mark Twain, Comstock, and more, all being wheeled by Puck and Uncle Sam to the Dumping Grounds. Hansen database #306.

did.unittitle: "The Decadence of the Wizard of Menlo Park--From the Phonograph to Polyform."

scopecontent.p: Puck (7:165), page 150-151, center. By J. Keppler, shows the press distracted by the electric light, while other dangerous products and activities are being overlooked due to Edison's shell game. Cartoon also appears in advertisement, see #560. Hansen database #238.

did.unittitle: "The Adulteration of Food."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:165), page 160, back cover. F. Opper, six scenes, analytical chemist is being paid off; "the big four" dancing together: The Grocer, the Doctor, the Undertaker, and the Sexton. Hansen database #307.

did.unittitle: "Captain of Overcrowded Steamer-'Room for One More.' Death 'I Shall Soon Be on Board'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:168), page 218, back cover. JAW (Wales). Death running toward gang plank, carrying suitcase labelled disease and accidents, wearing coffins as shoes. Copy 1. Issue has no editorial text on this. Hansen database #308.

did.unittitle: "Better Not Vaccinate than Vaccinate with Impure Virus."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:171), page 276, back cover. Signed J. Keppler, bull is in the Children's Ward. Hansen database #310.

did.unittitle: "Sunday Recreation as We Like It."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:173), page 316, back cover. J. A. Wales, people from factory lane rush off to the seashore despite condemnation by old-fashioned minister. Some preachers work the beach to find an audience in the crowded surf. A family picnics on the beach, visited by an angel labelled Health. This last scene is titled, "The Sermon on the Sands." With single color overlay. Hansen database #312.

did.unittitle: "The Republican Presidential Candidate Now on View. Charles A Dana 'Come and see! Two cents a sight! Great Sun Microscope! Magnifies 100,000,000,000 Diameters'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:179), front cover. Dana showing the "Sun" microscope to look at Garfield's record, with slide of sea creatures. Hansen database #156.

did.unittitle: "Inspecting the Democratic Curiosity Shop."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (7:182), page 456-457, center. By J. Keppler. Hancock (at sight of the Old Bourbon War Horse): "Great Scott! Am I to be Head of That?" Collection of artifacts includes KKK robes, Fugitive Slave Act, and Booth's pistol. Hansen database #239.

did.unittitle: "A Strange Case of Epizooty."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:189), page 108-109, center. By Keppler, shows sick donkey being treated with elixir from bottle labeled "Butler's Party Killer" and clyster labeled "solid south solution." Also includes barrels labeled "Allopathic" and "Homeopathic." and Hansen database #241.

did.unittitle: "The Three 'Dis-Graces'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:195), page 203, front cover. J. A. Wales, the bosses of New York, Brooklyn, and Philadelphia. Strong example of the wide limits of propriety in political cartoons. Hansen database #157.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Conception of Mr. Bergh's Steam Flogging Machine."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:196), front cover. By J. Keppler. Hansen database #158.

did.unittitle: "The Chosen People."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:196), page 226-227, center. By J. Keppler, Jewish peddler is thumbing his nose at three men kicking him out. "I have thriven on this sort of thing for Eighteen Centuries--Go on, gentlemen, Persecution helps de Pizness." On the ground are seen rocks labelled "prosecution," "Hebrew success at crime," "bigotry rampant." Judge Hilton keeps him out of Saratoga hotel; Corbin from Manhattan Beach; Bismarck, janitor of Hotel Berlin, which has sign "No Jews Allowed." Peddler's pants are labelled Endurance; his carried box of Prosperity held up by straps labelled sobriety and industry. The box contains literature, poetry, music, science, patriotism, statesmanship, and promissory notes. Hansen database #242.

did.unittitle: "Mr. Bergh's Dual Nature."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:204), page 378, back cover. Love and sympathy only for animals, hate for people. Hansen database #313.

did.unittitle: "The Quack Doctor's Last Dose."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:205), page 379, front cover. J. A. Wales, a man is being force-fed two pills labelled "Jacobus" and "Forster" by R. B. Hayes from a pill bottle labelled "Dr. Hayes' Ohio Prescription." Hansen database #159.

did.unittitle: "Wanted--A Bergh for Poor Humanity."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:205), page 394, back cover. Signed F. O. (Opper?) "Treat the Brutes like this--and they will look like this; and there will soon be a Society for the Suppression of Tenement Houses. Treat the Tenement House Dwellers like Brutes, and they will be happy." In lower scene, people are being scrubbed in a sanitary stable, with fresh water, soap, toilet sand, sunlight and the services of a doctor. Hansen database #314.

did.unittitle: "New York's Dirt and the Way to Get Rid of It. The citizens must literally take it into their own hands."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (8:207), page 430, back cover. By J. A. Wales. Hansen database #316.

did.unittitle: "It Looks Bad for the Bull."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:213), page 71, front cover of complete issue. J. A. Wales, the stalwarts as bull on a track trying to stop a locomotive of the administration party. Hansen database #160.

did.unittitle: "The Rent Question."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:213), page 78-79, center. By J. Keppler, Puck to Capitalist:--'Why not build little $1000 cottages and let them to our Laboring Classes at $12 a month, so that they may breathe pure air--it would be a paying investment? Capitalist:--'My dear fellow--they wouldn't live in them if I did. Even if they have to lodge over a Stable or a Gin mill, they won't leave the city!'" Copy 1. Hansen database #243.

did.unittitle: "The Rent Question."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:213), page 78-79, center, in complete issue. By J. Keppler, Puck to Capitalist:--'Why not build little $1000 cottages and let them to our Laboring Classes at $12 a month, so that they may breathe pure air--it would be a paying investment? / Capitalist:--'My dear fellow--they wouldn't live in them if I did. Even if they have to lodge over a Stable or a Gin mill, they won't leave the city!'" Copy 2. Hansen database #244.

did.unittitle: "Waiting."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:215), page 107, cover of complete issue. Senators (?) waiting outside U. S. Senate secured by padlock "Conkling & Mahone dead lock." Hansen database #161.

did.unittitle: "Between Two Rogues."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:215), page 114-115, centerfold in complete issue. By Keppler, showing New York City in rags escorted by New York City members of legislature and Rural member of New York state legislature. Skeletons on stoops are labelled diphtheria, smallpox, malaria, and fever; same names appear on a smoking barrel in the muddy street. Hansen database #245.

did.unittitle: "Disturbing the Neighbors."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:215), page 124, back cover of complete issue. Greece as Warlike baby watched over by a Turkey with a bottle of "Turkish soothing syrup." Hansen database #317.

did.unittitle: "The Mode and the Martyrs."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:216), page 142, back cover. Signed with unknown initials (J? T?). About tight corseting killing women; includes skeleton and cemetery. Hansen database #318.

did.unittitle: "A Hint to the Hebrews. How They May Make Themselves Independent of the Watering Place Hotels."

scopecontent.p: Puck (9:218), page 178, back cover. By F. Opper. Hansen database #319.

did.unittitle: "Bob Ingersoll, The 'Illustrious Infidel'."

did.unittitle: "No Interference with 'gentlemen's' amusements, you know."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:225), front cover. By F. Opper, Bergh at pigeon-shoot. Hansen database #162.

did.unittitle: "Another Matterhorn Catastrophe."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:227), back cover. By J. A. Wales, Ulysses S. Grant being pulled down by senatorial courtesy, while Blaine and Garfield look on. If date is correct, it's just prior to news of Garfield's being shot on July 2nd by Guiteau on next cover! Hansen database #320.

did.unittitle: "Where Mr. Field's Subscription List Would do the Most Good."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:228), front cover. J. A. Wales on injured railway worker in garret, satirizing a Mr. Field (philanthropist? industrialist?). Additional text on reverse. Hansen database #164.

did.unittitle: "A Mid-Summer Day's Dream. While Our Artist Sleeps, his Favorite Subjects Are Left to Do Justice to Themselves, and to Correct His Conceptions."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:231), page 388-389, center. By J. Keppler, shows artist asleep in hammock while subjects of his illustrations create more flattering self-portraits. Hansen database #250.

did.unittitle: "Ready for 1884."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (9:234), page 444, back cover. By J.A.W. (Wales), rejuvenated Tilden being dis-interred in cemetery by doctor with bag of medicine bottles (one marked elixir of life); doctor is carrying a stick which is too short for a cane and might be a large hypodermic needle (perhaps drawn by someone unfamiliar with this new device). Nearly a decade before Brown-Sequard. Hansen database #322.

did.unittitle: "Patent-Medicines."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (10:236), page 32, back cover. F. Opper. Hansen database #491.

did.unittitle: "The Gentler Sex--Charity for the Drunken Brother, Contempt for the Unfortunate Sister."

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:237), page 40-41, center. By J. A. Wales, shows woman with infant being turned away from a woman's home, while drunken man is helped by three well dressed women who offer him an abstinence pledge. Hansen database #251.

did.unittitle: "Getting Ahead of the Banks."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (10:247), page 193, front cover of complete issue. By F. Opper. Hansen database #167.

did.unittitle: "The Modern Moses."

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:247), page 200-201, center of complete issue. By F. Opper and J. Keppler (signed O & K). Uncle Sam with hook nose leading Jews from Oppression through sea of intolerance to western homes. Hansen database #254.

did.unittitle: "If This be Madness, there is Method in It!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (10:247), page 208, back cover of complete issue. By Gillam, regarding police protecting Guiteau from lynch mob. Hansen database #325.

did.unittitle: "Wanted--A Leader!"

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:248) page 209, cover of complete issue. By F. Opper., a blind lady Democracy sits on courthouse or capitol steps. Might be about veterans' widows' pensions--the subject of centerfold and of editorial comments. Hansen database #168.

did.unittitle: "The Pension Swindle."

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:248), page 216-217, center of complete issue. Unsigned. Hansen database #255.

did.unittitle: "Our `Infant Industry'."

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:248), page 224, back cover of complete issue. By Gillam, mocks puny protectionists beneath huge giant of industry. Hansen database #326.

did.unittitle: "Our Perennial Pauper."

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:249), page 225, front cover of complete issue. By Gillam, Grant is begging. Hansen database #169.

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:249), page 232-233, center of complete issue. By J. Keppler. Hansen database #256.

scopecontent.p: Puck (10:249), page 242, back cover of complete issue. By F. Opper, images relate both to Guiteau and to police shooting at mad dogs. Hansen database #327.

did.unittitle: 'The Moloch of Arctic Discovery. The World's Sacrifices to a Whim of Science."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (10:252), page 284-285, center. Shows hazards of arctic expeditions, with upper left inset of rich man warming his feet in front of a fire and lower right inset showing a sailor's suffering wife. This issue's cover is Guiteau on pedestal and back contains article about Guiteau. Compare to Puck Aug. 20, 1884. Hansen database #258.

did.unittitle: "Reason Against Unreason."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck , page 8-9, centerfold. By J. Keppler, Kepler thru Darwin; anti-Catholic, but some protestant preachers included too. Hansen database #259.

did.unittitle: "A Sunday Show--Profit for Pagan and Preacher."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (11:269), front cover. Ingersoll making money on stage by knocking out a puppet preacher with Bible in hand. Hansen database #172.

did.unittitle: "A Sun of the 19th Cent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (11:269), page 146, back cover. By F. Graetz, Darwin, compare to editorial comments on previous issue. Hansen database #328.

scopecontent.p: Puck (11:273), page 273, front cover. By F. Graetz. Kelly reviving an Indian corpse with a generator connected by a patronage wire. Hansen database #173.

did.unittitle: "Science, or Sport?--A Modern Spectacle After An Old Model."

scopecontent.p: Puck (11:273), page 202-203, centerfold. By J. Keppler, dying arctic explorers in gladiatorial ring, condemned by J.G. Bennett and Herald in the royal box. Hansen database #260.

did.unittitle: "For Sale Again."

scopecontent.p: Puck (11:286), page 405, front cover of complete issue. By F. Graetz. Hansen database #174.

scopecontent.p: Puck (11:286), page 420, back cover of complete issue. By F. Opper, American Indian Reservation. Hansen database #329.

did.unittitle: "The Great American Quack: A Good Remedy in Bad Hands."
