scopecontent.p: Puck (12:287), page 1, front cover. By Gillam, Wadsworth being touted by Herald editorials. Hansen database #175.

did.unittitle: "The Prohibition Movement--The Drug Store of the Future."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (12:289), page 48, back cover. By F. Graetz. Hansen database #330.

did.unittitle: "Discrimination: The Selfish Millionaire--How He is Taken Care of, and How He Takes Care of His Patrons."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (12:291), page 72-73, center. By F. Graetz, patrician being helped out of carriage, other scenes show problems at Inwood station, Spuyten Duyvil, injuries at 87th St. tunnel, and the hiring of boys to do men's work. Hansen database #262.

did.unittitle: "An Old Anecdote Illustrated."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (12:294), page 128, back cover. Seth Low shaving on left is imitated by Howard Carroll, a republican, as a shaving ape. Hansen database #331.

did.unittitle: "The Universal Church of the Future--From the Present Religious Outlook."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck , page 296-297, center. By J. Keppler, knowledge is power; know thyself; lecturers on science in church-like building. Framed portraits of Darwin, Kopernicus (sic), and T. Paine. One shelf holds "Books of Religious Reference" (through 1870s); other of Scientific Reference. Hansen database #263.

did.unittitle: "Gambling With Death. Too Enterprising American Capitalist 'Keep up the game! I can lose nothing--I'm heavily insured'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (12:307), page 328-329, center. By Gillam, he's sitting on bags of insurance money, playing dice with death on a coffin cover. Other scenes show shipwreck, riverboat explosion and fire, burned out tenement, railroad accident, hotel fire, and theater fire. Plus text. Hansen database #264.

did.unittitle: "An Apparatus By Means of Which Suicides Can Get the Better of the `Penal Code'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (12:309), page 353, cover. By F. Graetz, man seated in wooden chair has connected himself to guns, cannon, knives, charcoal fire, 5000 lb. weight, poison, and dynamite. A note reads: "Dear George, I can not marry you. Carri." Text on page 2: "The luxury of suicide is forbidden under the new penal code...We regard this as rather an interference with the rights of the citizen. If a man wishes to throw his life away, he ought to be permitted to do so. Lives that are thus carelessly disposed of are rarely of much value to the world..." Hansen database #176.

did.unittitle: "A Dreadful Attack of Presidential Fever in the U. S. Senate."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (:311), page 392-393, centerfold. By F. Opper, several named senators are soaking their feet, etc. Puck is dosing one with "anti-presidential quinine" (to get him out of the race?); reform bills in the wastebasket. Only thermometer shown is for water temperature, not fever. Hansen database #265.

did.unittitle: "An Appalling Attempt to Muzzle the Watch-Dog of Science."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:314), cover. Herbert Spencer as Lion in front of a museum or library. Hansen database #177.

did.unittitle: "Brooklyn's New Champion--The Old-Timers 'Knocked out' by the Prayer-Cure 'Pounder'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:320), cover. Old timers (preachers) knocked out by Reverend Dr. Monck as a boxer. Hansen database #178.

did.unittitle: "Old-School Etiquette. Dr. All O'Path 'Very sorry, madam, if your child must die; but you ought not to have called in a Homoeopath first'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:327), page 225, front cover of complete issue. By F. Graetz, Dr. All O'Path letting child because mother first called a Homeopath. Centerfold has more on the same topic. Hansen database #180.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Coaching Parade, 1883."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:327), page 232-233, center of complete issue. By F. Opper, many scenes each with several figures; in one, two coaches show young homeopaths spitting at older allopaths, who have pills, dose, and a large clyster used as a water cannon. Hansen database #266.

did.unittitle: "Switchmen at Loggerheads--Will There be Another Smash-Up?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:327), page 240, back cover of complete issue. By Gillam, politicians brawling over the tariff issue switch with a democracy (Democratic party?) train approaching danger, with Dana of New York Sun trying to stop the train. Hansen database #333.

did.unittitle: "Nursing Our Infant Industries."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:329), page 264-265, centerfold. By Graetz, these are clearly "baby nurses" not medical nurses. Hansen database #4441.

did.unittitle: "The Kind of 'Assisted Emigrant' We Can Not Afford To Admit."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (13:332), page 324, back cover. By Graetz, cholera on bowsprit. Hansen database #334.

did.unittitle: "Celestial wrath. China:--'I will make it HOT for you'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:340), page 12, cover. By F. Opper, French soldiers in rice paddy are facing hot sun plus fever, disease, and malaria. China is a face on a hot yellow sun, with a population of 500,000,000. Hansen database #181.

did.unittitle: "The Two Dromios. American Cowboy to British Nobleman (Villard Variety) 'Methinks, thou art my mirror'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:344), page 81, front cover of complete issue. Hansen database #972.

did.unittitle: "The Return Of The 'Prodigal Father' To The 'Puck' Office--Drawn by himself."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:344), page 88-89, center of complete issue. Important for history of cartooning, as it's Keppler drawing himself. Hansen database #973.

did.unittitle: "John Roach's Hospital For Our Robesonized Navy."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:344), page 96, back cover of complete issue. Hospital, medicine bottles, bandages, and medicine spoons. Ward is $8 per day board with sign "No Charity Patients." Hansen database #534.

did.unittitle: "Rotten To The Core. Uncle Sam 'I suppose I've got to have the derned thing out--only pull it easy'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:348), page 145, front cover of a complete issue. Tooth, teeth, dentist, laughing gas, dental association, and dental tool that looks like a cutting pliers. Hansen database #974.

did.unittitle: "In The Jaws Of Death.--A Cold Day For The Independent Party."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:348), page 152-153, center of a complete issue. Hansen database #537.

did.unittitle: "Intoxicated With His Own glory. His supports are giving way--can he stand alone?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (14:348), page 160, back cover of a complete issue. Hansen database #975.

did.unittitle: "Look Before You Eat--And See If You Can Discover Any Unadulterated Food."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:366), page 48, cover. By F. Opper, man with chemical instruments and microscope on dinner table. Additional text page 18. Hansen database #183.

did.unittitle: "Bismarck's Fury: [Ambassador] Sargent 'Shoo, fly--don't bodder me'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:367), page 48, back. By F. Graetz, another Graetz cartoon in this topic, i.e. Uncle Sam protecting his pig, appeared on August 15, 1883. Hansen database #291.

did.unittitle: "The Alchemist of the Past, and the Alchemist of the Present."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:369), page 80, back. By F. Graetz, Dr. Cashdown Mixer, Analytical Chemist, who charges for analysis of coffee, flour, oilymargarine, honey, showing presence of adulterants in each. Hansen database #336.

did.unittitle: "The National Dime Museum--Will be Run During the Presidential Campaign."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:371), page 104-105, center. By Gillam, includes tattooed man, a stuffed Tammany tiger killed by Roosevelt, wax group of three heroes who perished in an attempt to reach the pole of tariff reform, Jefferson Davis in a dress, wild zulu on the warpath, and (Barnum's) What Is It?. Two extra pages attached and text. Hansen database #267.

did.unittitle: "A Transparent Lie."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:371), page 112, back. By F. Graetz, shows blazing fire upstairs in "St. Phosphorus Flats--Absolutely Fireproof---Elevator to Eleventh Floor." Fabulous use of yellow and red ink, with some dark blue in sky. The page almost glows. Hansen database #337.

did.unittitle: "A Wall Street Cleaner."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:376), page 177, front cover of a complete issue. Shows street cleaning machine. Similar or same machine in Harper's Weekly November 12, 1881. Hansen database #547.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Plan To Relieve The Country Of Two Embarrassments--Give Grant The Surplus, And Let Him Spend It On A Little Court Of His Own."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:376), page 184-185, center of a complete issue. Hansen database #976.

did.unittitle: "A New Version of The Pied Piper of Hamelin--The Children Refuse to Be Charmed."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:376), back cover of a complete issue. Hansen database #548.

did.unittitle: "An Unexpected Chance for a Very Sick Party."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:379), page 240, back. By F. Opper, Democratic Party in bed with softening of the brain, case being discussed by three doctors: Dana, Waterson, and S.J.T. Compare follow-up on June 25th. Hansen database #338.

did.unittitle: "The Very Sick [Democratic] Party Becomes Convalescent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:381), page 272, back. By J. Keppler, refers to earlier cover on June 11th. Copy 1. Hansen database #339.

did.unittitle: "The Very Sick [Democratic] Party Becomes Convalescent."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:381), page 272, back. By J. Keppler, refers to earlier cover on June 11th. Copy 2. Hansen database #374.

did.unittitle: "A Picture for the Foes of Sunday Concerts: the Clergyman's Leisure and the Poor Man's Holiday."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:385), page 336, back. Lower half shows poor family in a garret; upper shows clergyman going to Europe where there are alpine hikes and open-air concerts. Hansen database #340.

did.unittitle: "The World's Morgue: When will there be an End to these Sacrifices to Sham Science?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (15:389), page 395. By F. Graetz black and white cartoon of North Pole. Shows wrecked ships, a polar bear, and tombstones marking Danish, French, English, Swedish, American, and German victims. Compare Puck centerfold January 4, 1882 (#258) also Puck complete page 118 October 26, 1881. Hansen database #407.

did.unittitle: "Narcissus; or, the Man Who Was Mashed On Himself."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:393), page 48, back cover. By Gillam, shows the tattooed man lying next to a pool with lily ponds. Beautiful example of the famous Tattooed Man series. Hansen database #341.

did.unittitle: "Our Merciless Millionaire. Vanderbilt.--'The Public Be--Doctored'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:399), page 129, front cover of complete issue. Vanderbilt giving money to P & S, by F. Opper. Hansen database #184.

did.unittitle: "The Honor of the Country in Danger."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:399), page 136-137, centerfold of complete issue. By Gillam, a clown named Bargain with Blaine offers a tattooed Blaine the presidential sacred chair with ghosts of Lincoln and Washington watching. Hansen database #268.

did.unittitle: "A Sacrifice to the Political Wolf. Republican Desperation and the Peril of New York City."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:399), page 144, back cover of complete issue. Throwing New York City (a child) out of a sleigh to a wolf called Tammany. Hansen database #342.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:402), cover. By Gillam, shows tattooed man butt up in the water next to a dog (?) also butt up, labelled "We claim everything, Me & Jack," calling from a soap campaign; bar is labelled "hurrah soap--to remove tattoo." This cover refers back to front cover of July 2, 1884, "Me and Jack," showing Blaine and a dog sitting on a rotting plank. A later cover picks up image again: a Wet dog on plank, front cover (page 225 of Vol. 17), on June 10, 1885. Hansen database #186.

did.unittitle: "The Transfusion of Blood--May the Operation Prove a Success!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:404), page 216-217, center. The English edition, by J. Keppler, blood transfusion from Independent to Democracy, being timed by Puck with six observers, some of whom are editors, others perhaps politicians, but none are named. Copy 2 (Copy 1 is German version, Hansen #269). Hansen database #240.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Hint for 'Hospital Sunday'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:407), page 257, front cover of complete issue. By J. Keppler, with a collection can and a woman handing flowers to a boy on crutches with a hospital ward in the background. "Here Christian, Jew, & Pagan meet..." Copy 1. Hansen database #187.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Hint for 'Hospital Sunday'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:407), page 257, front cover of complete issue. By J. Keppler, with a collection can and a woman handing flowers to a boy on crutches with a hospital ward in the background. "Here Christian, Jew, & Pagan meet..." Copy 2. Hansen database #188.

did.unittitle: "A moment of Anxiety--Who is Going to Get Left?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:407), page 264-265, center of complete issue. Cleveland and many other figures by fireplace wondering about stockings and Santa. Hansen database #270.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Revised Library of 'Juvenile Fiction'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:407), page 272, back cover of complete issue. Six vignettes by Opper, "Designed to suit the taste of a rising generation." Hansen database #343.

did.unittitle: "Our Mutual Friend."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (16:409), page 289, cover. By J. Keppler, shows the cemetery manager (Sexton) and the doctor both pleased with the confectionery's use of poisonous additives as coloring agents: chrome green, red lead, arsenic, chrome yellow, vermillion, verdigris, and glucose. Hansen database #190.

did.unittitle: "The Duty That Lies Near."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:422), page 96, back cover. By Zimmerman, Puck: "The new parks are a good thing, Mr. Grace, but suppose we begin by making 'breathing spaces' of these dirty streets." Hansen database #344.

did.unittitle: "Ulysses S. Grant."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:422), supplement. By J. Keppler, portrait of Ulysses S. Grant, surrounded by scenes. Trimmed on top, with text. Hansen database #408.

did.unittitle: "Puck's Political Faith Cure."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:428), page 192, back cover. By F. Opper, about civil service reform. Since cure is believing in honest government, no medical instruments are shown except for cast-away crutches. Hansen database #346.

did.unittitle: "Sheol."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:427), page 200-201, center. By J. Keppler, includes Darwin, Galileo, Socrates, Voltaire, Jefferson, Hypatia, Goethe, Mill, George Sand, and Offenbach. Hansen database #271.

did.unittitle: "The Old Attempt. Mr. Beecher is Trying to Bridge the Chasm between the Old Orthodoxy and Science with his Little Series of 'Evolution Sermons.' We Wish Him Luck; but We Don't Feel Hopeful."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:430), page 224, back cover. By Zimmerman, has Mt. Rushmore-like faces of Darwin, Haeckel, Huxley, Tyndall, Spencer, Copernicus, and Spinoza. Hansen database #347.

did.unittitle: "Scientists Assert that All Diseases Can be Prevented by Inoculation."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (17:433), page 264-265, centerfold. By J. Keppler, "Now, my friends, step right up and be vaccinated for all forms of disease to which bank officials are liable." Bottles labeled: "lymph from swindling bank presidents," "vaccine from speculating bank teller" "virus from defaulting bank cashier," "virus vaccine from light-fingered scrubwoman," "virus from corruptible janitor," "lymph from embezzling bank clerk," "virus from receiver of stolen goods," and "virus from thieving office boy." Hansen database #272.

did.unittitle: "The Opium-Joint of the Republican 'Irreconcilables'--A Cheap Way of Being Happy."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (18:446), page 56-57, center. By Zimmerman, opium den. Hansen database #273.

did.unittitle: "At It Again! Through the Ballot-box to the Constitution."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (18:454), page 177, front cover of a complete issue. By Zimmerman, Pope Leo XIII is reaching to tear down the provision about not establishing religion. Striking example of political caricature, also a nice design and an (early?) Zimmerman. Hansen database #977.

did.unittitle: "A Bogus Cremation for the Benefit of the Life-Long Democrats."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (18:454), page184-185, center of a complete issue. "Uncremated Mugwump (from outside) 'If those old Bourbons take that…me, they'll be a little startled when they find out that I'm alive-and kicking'!" Cremation, but no technical information on cremation is provided in the picture. Hansen database #580.

did.unittitle: "Why The Snow Failed."

scopecontent.p: Puck (18:454), page 192, back cover of a complete issue. "Dunstan Kirke Logan 'Twenty years ago--Disgusted Populace--Oh, Chestnut'!!!" Hansen database #978.

did.unittitle: "Rough On America's Great Cartoonists. Have mercy on us, Grim Destroyer! You have taken away most of our best subjects already; if you keep on this way, our occupation will be gone!"

did.unittitle: "It Isn't The Cowl That Makes The Monk."

By K., shows Puck offering "Free Trade Elixir" to a politician (Blaine?) who's trying to use a bellows marked "wind" to revive a fainted Columbia wrapped in a ribbon marked South American Trade. Another man uses a fan marked Pan-American Congress. Three others (doctors?) look on skeptically at politician's efforts. Hansen database #362.

did.unittitle: "Dangerous Doctors for a Desperate Case."

scopecontent.p: Puck (28:718), page 276-277, centerfold. A sick Republican elephant is to receive an injection from James G. Blaine. Hansen database #283.

did.unittitle: "No Privacy is Sacred from Him [a reporter]; Mr. Penny-a-liner 'What do I want here? Don't you know that no private citizen has any rights that the Sensation Press is bound to respect'?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (29:752), page 377, cover. By F. Opper, related article inside of cover mentions the impropriety of attention to Blaine's hypochondriacal sensitiveness, but image on cover is not Blaine; the reporter carries a Textbook on Bright's Disease. Hansen database #210.

did.unittitle: "The Elixir of Life; or, the Philosopher's Discovery. --A Holiday Fantasy."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (30:773), page 332, back cover. By F. Opper, six scenes where the assistant fools the Philosopher by surreptitiously adding whiskey to the elixir. Hansen database #363.

did.unittitle: "The Frankenstein of the Snap Convention."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck , page 24-25, centerfold. Political cartoon by C. J. Taylor, Hill is being trampled by a composite monster, some of whose parts are presidential aspirations, snap convention, liquor interest, 1888, spoils, Tammany, aqueduct scandals, etc. Image has no reference to Mary Shelley, but does confirm two things: that Frankenstein is a recognized metaphor and that Frankenstein is still the monster's creator, not yet the monster. Title is ambiguous, but subtitle isn't: "Carried away by an insolent ambition for a power to which had no right to aspire, Frankenstein created a monster, which, designed to do his bidding and to serve him as a slave, became in the end his master and the implement employed by Fate to bring him to destruction." Hansen database #2510.

did.unittitle: "He's Got to Take It!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (31:791), page 161, front cover. By Dalrymple, Republican party is giving huge spoon of Harrison Re-Nomination Medicine to Tom Platt. Hansen database #211.

did.unittitle: "The Point of View."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (31:791), page 162, reverse of front cover. Black and white doctor cartoon. Patient: the examination seems to have delighted you, doctor. I judge from your happy countenance that you can save my life. Dr. Sawbones "I cannot promise you that; but we must perform a number of most interesting operations on you." Hansen database #418.

did.unittitle: "A Hopeless Misalliance. Matchmaker Sherman 'I've done my best to keep 'em united, but it's no use--it's a case of utter incompatibility'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (32:826), page 311, front cover. By J. Taylor, John Sherman's Gold & Silver Certificate of Marriage. Shows a male coin marked "par" departing for Europe, while a crying female marked "60-cent dollar" cries in a chair. She uses Bland's smelling salts, Jones Elixir of Life, Stewart's tonic, purely western, and B. Russel's conference fizz. Hansen database #212.

did.unittitle: "A Final Autopsy. Verdict--Died from Swallowing an Utterly Indigestible Object."

scopecontent.p: Puck (32:832), page 409, front cover. By W. A. Rogers, Reid and Harrison with knife and saw have cut open the GOP elephant and found the McKinley Bill inside. Hansen database #213.

did.unittitle: "The Decline of Chivalry. Puck Suggests that Perhaps the Ladies Themselves are the Cause of it."

scopecontent.p: Puck (33:848), back. By F. Opper, includes lady doctor and lady dentist amongst the six vignettes. Hansen database #364.

did.unittitle: "Easing Her Last Days."

did.unittitle: "The Big Fireman and the Silly Little Newsboys. While he puts out the flames caused by Republican mismanagement, they try to frighten the country about it."

scopecontent.p: Puck (33:857), page 392-393, centerfold. President Cleveland as firefighter, surrounded by editors as newsboys hawking false news about him. Hansen database #284.

did.unittitle: "Uncle Sam's Dismal Swamp. It Will Have to be Drained to Get Rid of the Noxious Miasmas that Arise From It."

scopecontent.p: Puck (34:871), page 200-201, centerfold. By Dalrymple, includes Tweedism, Crokerism, Tannerism, McLaughlinism, etc. Spoils system as malarial morass appeared in center of August 27, 1884, and Dismal Swamp was on June 22, 1892 cover. Hansen database #285.

did.unittitle: "Tariff Reform Tonic. Uncle Sam Has only Had One Sip, but it has Already Done him Good."

scopecontent.p: Puck (36:920), front cover. By Dalrymple. Hansen database #217.

Dr. Parkhurst (with saw) is working on a supine and sick Tammany tiger and directing Dr. Schurz (sharpening a knife) and Dr. Grace. All three wear white aprons or towels. Nearby toolbox shows tools and "Giant Powder--Reform Brand." Copy 1. Hansen database #218.

did.unittitle: "Make a Complete Job of It, This Time! Cut Him Up Into Small Pieces;--Don't Let Any of His Nine Lives Get Away!"

scopecontent.p: Puck (38:974), page 184-185, centerfold of complete issue. By Dalrymple, China is being fed spoons of ultimatum pills by England, Russia, and Japan from international pill box. Hansen database #286.

did.unittitle: "Love at First Sight."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (38:974), back cover of complete issue. By Ehrhart, European prince pursues American girl for her money. Hansen database #366.

did.unittitle: "Why the First Meeting of the 'Advanced Women's Club' Was a Failure."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (38:983), page 368, back cover. By F. Opper, six scenes: #1 speaker Mrs. Prof. Brayneford is introduced, #2-5 various criticisms which cause individual women to walk out, #6 "In conclusion, I would say that skirts which touch the ground are a menace to health, bringing microbes from the streets to our homes and firesides." Last guest departs, leaving only speakers and a hostess in tears. Coloring in blue, green, yellow, and orangey-red is nice, though format is not dramatic nor is design original. Hansen database #367.

did.unittitle: "Science's Next Stride with the 'X Rays.' No Doubt We Shall Soon Be Able to See Through Things, as Well as to Photograph Through Them."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (39:992), back cover. By F. Opper, six vignettes. Hansen database #368.

did.unittitle: "With the Compliments of the Season. Puck 'Allow me, Major, to congratulate you on your coming inauguration, and to offer you this little Christmas gift, to decorate your private office in the White House'."

scopecontent.p: Puck (40:1033), front cover of complete issue. By J. Taylor, Puck congratulating, "Major" (McKinley?), on coming inauguration, offering a Christmas gift of a sign "Public Office is a Public Trust." Hansen database #220.

did.unittitle: "Convalescent. Uncle Sam (to his three eminent doctors of different schools) 'Say, gentlemen, I'm getting over my prostration so fast that all I need is a good dose of that bottle on the table, and you can throw all this stuff here out of the window'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (40:1033), center of complete issue. By Dalrymple, Uncle Sam feeling good, asking doctors Cleveland, McKinley, and Reed for currency reform medicine, and abandoning Bryan's remedies, shown on the tables as bottles, pills, electrical machine, and air/water thermometer. Hansen database #287.

did.unittitle: "Getting Troublesome Again. Uncle Sam 'I guess he won't stop howling till I give him enough Protection Soothing Syrup to burst him'!"

scopecontent.p: Puck (40:1034), front cover. By Dalrymple, Uncle Sam in night shirt, baby is infant industries. Hansen database #221.

did.unittitle: "He's Back Again!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck ( :1036), centerfold. By J. S. Pughe, shows protection glutton as a huge pig stomping through the legislature. Hansen database #288.

did.unittitle: "Let Boss Platt Beware. Father Knickerbocker is in Training for This 'Sandow Act'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (41:1057), front cover. By Keppler (Jr.). Hansen database #222.

did.unittitle: "The Modern Plague. The Mills are Starting Up Again for Next Year's Visitation."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (42:1072), page 8-9, center of complete issue. By Dalrymple, shows a harried Uncle Sam in a hammock is swatting off the plague of new diploma-bearing graduates, portrayed as insects, from medical college, law school, and theological institute. One doctor carries a huge syringe, like a stinger. Front cover by Keppler is about Reed, Dingley Tariff, and the Railroad Lobby (looking like Simon Legree). Back by F. Opper (II?) has six scenes of henpecked husbands through the ages. Hansen database #693.

scopecontent.p: Puck (53:1355), centerfold. Unsigned, center cartoon spoofs doctors, surrounded by seven smaller cartoons about other "specialists." Hansen database #290.

did.unittitle: "The Finishing Touch."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (54:1382), cover. By Ehrhart, "The Nurse.--The Doctor has come to take your temperature. The Convalescent.-- I suppose it's all I have left." Medications etc. are seen on table, but no thermometer is visible! Hansen database #228.

did.unittitle: "The New Baby."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (61:1561), front cover. Signed Kep (Keppler Jr.?), doctor in top hat is leaving, with bag labeled Interstate Commerce Commission, M.D.; in bed with night-capped Corporate Corruption is new baby Railroad Scandal, and standing next to bed is older baby, Life insurance Scandal. On the table is bottle of Legislation Drops, and a metal bank for "other people's pennies." Hansen database #798.

did.unittitle: Puck (German)
"Für die ehrenwerthen Schulräthe."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German ), page 256, back cover. Translation is "For the honorable school councilors/supervisors." Three-color lithograph by Joseph Keppler. Subject is the foul air in schools, causing illness among children. The English and German editions were generally published at the same time with the same cartoons. Note that all the words inside the image are in English, while the caption below the border is in German. Main words in the image are "Foul Air" and "Bad Sewage." The teacher is a figure of Death, and a member of the Board of Education at the doorway is holding his nose. Hansen database #3811

did.unittitle: "Zur Autopsy-Manie der Lebens-Versucherungs-Gesellschaften."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , back cover. Translated by Hansen as "The Popular American Life Insurance Autopsy." Puck (speaking to Life Insurance Agent) "Is this the sort of treatment you insure your late customers?" Hansen database #3893.

did.unittitle: "Die New Yorker Strassenreinigungs-Frage."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , back cover. J. A. Wales, with citizens doing work of the Street Cleaning Bureau. The English version is Hansen database #316. Hansen database #315.

did.unittitle: "Ein Hundefang, wie wir ihn sehen möchten."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , centerfold. By Keppler. Sign on back wall: "All unfaithful politicians captured will be drowned here, without mercy." Cage at the right, operated by Uncle Sam as Columbia's direction, show condemned curs in the political pound ready to be hoisted into the river. (English edition citation is (9:224), page 274-275, "A dog round-up, as we'd like to see it.") Hansen database #246.

did.unittitle: "Un-Heilkünstler."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , page 272-273, centerfold. By J. Keppler. A quack at top center, surrounded by Indian herb cure, the magnetic cure, the sulphur bath, the water cure, etcetera. At bottom center, a happy skeleton dressed as death dances between a doctor and a pharmacist. At upper right, a drug store "has something for anything." Title of English version is "Death's-Head Doctors: Many Paths to the Grave." Hansen database #249.

did.unittitle: "Auf Dem Wege Zum Bicycle-Turnier. 'Puck on Wheels' mit 'all the Modern Improvements'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) (7:350), cover. By F. Opper, Puck on bicycle, at the head of line of cyclists, carrying crutches, splints, bandages, arnica, and a Medical and Surgical Directory. Hansen database #179.

did.unittitle: "Eine neue Variante einer alten Anekdote. Onkel Sam zu Bismark 'Haust Du mein Schwein, hau' ich Deinem Wein'!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , back cover. By F. Graetz, "A New Variant of an old Anecdote." English version title is "Tit for Tat." Uncle Sam to Bismarck: "If you hit my swine, I'll have a crack at your wine!" On reciprocal boycott of American pork and German wine. Wine bottle contains vinegar, sugar of lead, aniline dyes, and glucose. Hansen database #335.

did.unittitle: "Die Blut-Transfusion--Moege die Operation von Erfolg sein!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , center, page 184-185. By J. Keppler, "May the Operation Prove ..." Shows blood transfusion from Independent to Democracy, being timed by Puck with six observers, some of whom are editors, others perhaps politicians, but none are named. Copy 1. (Copy 2 is English, page 216-217.) Hansen database #269.

did.unittitle: "Zwei Jünger Aesculap's, oder: Die Wichtigkeit des Bartes."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Puck (German) , back cover. By F. Opper, translation "Two Young Aesculaps, or the importance of the Beard." Hardworking student who becomes a Dr. has no patients, while carousing fellow who grows a beard has a full waiting room. He's seen taking a lady's pulse with pocket watch. A young black boy acts as door-keeper of the doctor's study. No medical accoutrements in the successful doctor's rooms; industrious student had skull, anatomical drawings, and piles of books in his room. Hansen database #358.

It measures approx. 13 x 16.5, picture area measures 9.5 x 13. #2873b = Typogravure print from 1895, "Vaccination in the Hospital at Trousseau" by A. Brouillet, Printed by Boussod, Valedon & Cie.; image 9 x 11.75, paper 11 x 15 inches. Printed on heavier smoother paper. Comes from an art magazine folio; has text on back. Hansen database #2873.

did.unittitle: "Pasteur -- The Famous French Chemist, One of the World's Practical Benefactors."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Household (28:11), page 11, in a complete issue. Article with photograph leads with Pasteur treatment for rabies, also mentions spontaneous generation and germ theory. Draws on a sketch in the New York Tribune . In closing: "We shall do well to keep in mind Pasteur's saying: `It is in the power of man to cause all parasitic maladies to disappear from the world.' This is his best epitaph; for the world owes all that may come of the `germ theory' in the future to Louis Pasteur." Magazine was established 1868 in Boston, Massachussetts, and still published there, the paper's masthead states it is devoted to the interests of the American housewife. Hansen database #3343

did.unittitle: "Their Majesties and the Light Cure: King Edward and Queen Alexandra Visiting Patients in the Finsen Light Room at the London Hospital."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Illustrated London News , center, page 950-951. Drawn by A. Forestier, caption notes the royals visited on June 11 to open a new wing and a new Finsen Light Room. "Of the Danish physician's method of curing lupus, the Queen is the patron and pioneer in England. Her majesty presented the first lamp to the London Hospital in 1900." Two aspects of interest are Finsen Light's popularity at the time and a further transformation of the long tradition of the royal touch. Here it's their patronage of science and their funding that does the healing, but it's still their touch. Is this a change from or a continuity with royalty's appearances at cholera hospitals, where there was little therapy, beyond calming down a frightened public by gestures like these? Also see Image #1939. Hansen database #147.

did.unittitle: "Gardien de sa Propre Statue."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: L'Illustration: Journal Universel Hebdomadaire (71:3688), front cover of complete issue. Full page photograph (8.5 x 11 inches) of Jean-Baptiste Jupille in uniform next to a sculpture in the garden of the Pasteur Institute. Sculpture created by Émile Louis Truffot in 1887 depicts Pasteur fighting off a rabid dog. Jupille was the second person Pasteur treated for rabies in 1885 (Joseph Meister was the first) and became an employee of the Pasteur Institute as a guard or concierge. This copy of the issue has 16 pages in front and in back of additional advertising material. A story on the building of the Panama Canal has some unusual early color photographs. Hansen database #4454

did.unittitle: "Vaccination from the Calf."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: London Graphic (27:685), page 37, in complete issue. Two sketches, text on page 34. Taking lymph from the calf and Vaccinating infants. This is Fig. 1, inset in upper left corner of Fig. 2 (upper sketch). Fig. 1 shows the peculiar pattern of punctures so that there will remain evidence that this was lymph from the calf, rather than humanized lymph. Brief text also notes the lack of any suffering on the animal's part, saying it often sleeps during the procedure. Hansen database #1918.

did.unittitle: "The Spanish Honor Cure."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Merry Maker (7:9), cover of complete issue. Formatted and laid out like the contemporary Life magazine, cartoon showing Uncle Sam shooting cannon balls each labeled "pill" into the mouth of a monkey-like toreador. Uncle Sam has no stethoscope or doctor's bag, so the pills are the only medical signifier. Masthead appears on page 16. "The Merry Maker and American House and Home: A journal of Humorous Literature, published monthly by the House and Home publishing Co., 154 Nassau Street, New York City." Subscription is 50 cents a year in advance. Back cover has a story without words in six scenes. "The Rise and Progress of a Mad Dog Scare" including panicked people and a policeman shooting wildly. In the end a calm dog walks away. Signed "Phillips--Dunk." Hansen database #4450

did.unittitle: "Blindness Vanishes Before the March of Modern Science"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Herald, Sunday edition, page 2, section 3, full page. Banner headline and large article on upper half of page, with three images of eyeballs and pictures of Dr. S. Josephine Baker and of Dr. John Herbert Claiborne. The triumphs are ophthalmia neonatorum prevention by Crede's method, shown by the great reduction in cases in New York City under new Division of Child Hygiene and trachoma. There's also much about dim light and small type; Herald gets commendation for using larger type. Hansen database #4046.

did.unittitle: "New York - The Pasteur Institute for Treating Persons Bitten by Dogs."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Once a Week , page 12. Six vignettes in a half-page engraving, including portrait of Dr. Gibier and an inoculating scene with patient, Gibier, and two assistants. No whites except for aprons on lab dieners. Hansen database #4275

did.unittitle: "Dr. Koch's Discovery."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Once a Week (6:12), page 11. Article with a drawing of a patient with two doctors, "Dr. Bergmann showing effect of lymph." Article reports that in New York City there are now 116 cases under experimental treatment at 9 hospitals. It lists hospitals and number of cases ranging from 4 to 29. It mentions that five physicians went to Germany for training and returned on December 22 with enough lymph for an estimated 30,000 inoculations. Hansen database #4281

did.unittitle: "La peste en Mandchourie."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Le Petit Journal, Supplement Illustré , page 1. Bright color cover illustration of death in red robe with scythe hovering over hordes fleeing a burning village behind them. There is text on page 58 (not in folder) on this topic as well as a longer article about plague in Europe in the past. (ca. 12 x 16 in.) During the winter of 1910 to 1911, an explosive epidemic of pneumonic plague raged in Manchuria and northern China. At the time, this region was divided into zones of influence, and each zone was controlled by a European power or Japan, all of which feared that plague would compromise their commercial interests. The Manchurian epidemic of 1910 to 1911 undoubtedly stressed an already unstable Chinese government and contributed to undermining the political integrity of the 250-year-old Qing empire, which fell the following autumn. The epidemic began in September 1910 and ended the following April. During the peak 4 months, from October through January, pneumonic plague killed more than 50,000 people. Hansen database #3710

did.unittitle: "How a Woman Saves a City's Babies"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pittsburgh Sunday Post , single page. Large news feature with photo montage concerning Dr. S. Josephine Baker, Director of Division of Child Hygiene, New York Department of Health. Original imprint, some headlines done in red ink and red also used for some photo borders. Approximately 23 x 16, broadsheet size. Hansen database #4154.

did.unittitle: "An Anatomical Interruption."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Police Gazette (39:223), cover illustration of a complete issue. "How the legal spouse of a fashionable physician expressed her disapprobation of the course of treatment her husband subjected his pretty patients to; N. Y. City." He's in fancy dark suit (no medical items present) caressing the chin of a fancy young lady with feathered hat and long train, while his wife is behind him ready to crown him with a skull (cranium and face without lower jaw). This image reconfirms that physicians were not yet distinguished visually by any accoutrements. Related story is on page 7. The Police Gazette was a "sporting paper" for young male readers, with scandal and sensation, but sometimes it commented on medical and scientific items repeated from general newspapers. Also includes half page story on Guiteau on page 6. Hansen database #2814

did.unittitle: "The Hoodoo Doctor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Public Herald ( :116), front page of complete issue. Philadelphia publication, slogan on masthead: "Truth and Justice. The Foe of Fraud and Corruption." Two of four front-page columns are about quack doctors, including a winning malpractice lawsuit. Hansen database #69

did.unittitle: "Inhalation of Ether in Surgery."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Spirit of the Times (New York), page 15, in complete issue. Rare early report on ether in a popular weekly sports newspaper that covers horse racing, hunting, fishing, cricket, boat racing, other sporting news, literature, stage news, and some general news. Article appears to be credited to the London Athenaeum, but citations at the top are to The Lancet , January 16 and 23, Medical Gazette , January 22, and Medical Times , January 17 and 23. Hansen database #3516

did.unittitle: Later Publications

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged by publication title, date, and item title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes partial and complete issues of numerous 20th and 21st century periodical publications including Life , Look , Saturday Evening Post , Newsweek , Time , and U.S. News and World Report . Many items in the series are photographic essays on medical issues, medical and scientific advancements, and noted medical and public health practitioners. Subjects of stories include polio, cancer, transplants, artificial organs, medicine in wartime, midwifery, contraception, fertility, mental health, gender, sexuality, and medical ethics. The series also includes advertisements using medical imagery.

scopecontent.p: Life (16:18), pages 60-67. Science, tropical medicine, double-page map of the major tropical diseases. Elephantiasis, sleeping sickness and leprosy and many more. Living chick embryos produce new vaccines. Prevention is surest of all cures. Color photos and diagrams.

did.unittitle: "About Penicillin"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (16:23), page 7. "Squibb, a Name you can trust," Squibb advertisement. All black and white, petri dish plus lab photograph. Big Life story on penicillin is July 17th.

did.unittitle: "Quinine: Two young chemists end a century's search by making drug synthetically from coal tar"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (16:23), pages 85-88. Science, molecular association; Quinine; Doering, William von Eggers; Woodward, R. B., 1917-1979, by Fritz Goro (featured photographer on contents page). This is clearly a source for the story in Marvels of Science, #2, April 1946, pages 28-31, since at least two frames copy photographs from LIFE.

did.unittitle: Wyeth advertisement

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (16:23), page 119, color page. "'17 Babies this week - and he hasn't seen his own grandson'!" a maternity nurse says to another.

did.unittitle: "Penicillin: Mass production of drug replaces slow laboratory methods to meet military needs and provide a limited civilian supply"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (17:3), pages 57-61. Molds, botany, penicillin. Includes color pictures of mold. Many photographs, including the Terre Haute Indiana Penicillin Plant.

did.unittitle: "The Doctor's Here!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (17:14), single sheet. Advertisement by United States Rubber Company. One of four such ads in this issue using doctors and childbirth imagery to sell something else. Hansen database #4466

did.unittitle: "Are more boy babies born in wartime?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (17:14), page 24, single sheet. Full page advertisement by The Prudential. One of four such ads in this issue using doctors and childbirth imagery to sell something else. Large photograph of obstetric nurse presenting viewer with two newborns. Hansen database #4465

did.unittitle: "Mothers, it's back--at new low price!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (17:18), page 80, single sheet. Advertisement for Mennen Antiseptic Baby Oil, photographs of doctor, nurse, and mother with a quotation from each. One of four such advertisements in this issue using doctors and childbirth imagery to sell something else. Hansen database #4468

did.unittitle: "From now on, Mother, they pin their faith on you"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (17:18), page 82, single sheet. Advertisement by Scot Tissue, large photograph shows new father, masked, helping a doctor in scrubs put a bandage on a newborn. Father is holding small bottle with dropper. Not clear if this is antiseptic for umbilical cord or silver nitrate for the baby's eyes. Second photo shows mother with a mask giving a bottle to her baby. Caption says "A Scot Tissue emergency mask--shown in the picture above--has two practical merits. It is used only once and is instantly disposable." One of four such ads in this issue using doctors and childbirth imagery to sell something else. Hansen database #4467

did.unittitle: "The Famous Iwo Flag-Raising"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (18:13), pages 17-18. "Life's Reports," story about the background and staging of the famous photo at Iwo Jima and the comparisons made with Leutze painting, which was posed by models in Germany, for Washington crossing the Delaware.

did.unittitle: "Blood: Research has made it raw material for spectacular new products"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (18:13), pages 69-75. Science, blood, medical research, medicine, products.

did.unittitle: "A Prayer Is Answered: Shell Research opens the way to great increases in production of the miracle drug PENICILLIN"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (18:20), page 47. Advertisement for Shell Oil, shows handsome, generic military man in clean pressed uniform walking toward camera away from a burning ruin, with crutches also dropped on the ground behind him. Hansen database #4137

did.unittitle: "Prison Malaria: Convicts expose themselves to disease so doctors can study it"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (18:23), pages 43-44, 46. Medicine, malaria; medical research, prisoners.

did.unittitle: "Germs"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (19:19), pages 65-70. "Medical science helps the body in its fight against six great groups." Biological products, diseases, communicable diseases, microorganisms, pathogenic.

did.unittitle: "Girls' Medical School"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (19:24), pages 91-94. "Woman's medical college of Pennsylvania, now 95 years old, is the only one in the U.S." Philadelphia, full page photograph of woman using dog for EKG(?)

did.unittitle: Squibb advertisement

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (20:4), page 86. "Your Health and Victories in Medical Research." Victories are rabies, foxglove for heart conditions, penicillin, and influenza vaccine. "Defeating rabies...let no one deprive a boy of the character-building companionship of his dog; foxglove plant...; mother of penicillin is the mycelium ...rolling like a carpet off a large rotary filter in the Squibb penicillin plant; marching against flu...2000 eggs per day... influenza vaccine...soon for use by civilian doctors." Pasteur in advertisement. Hansen database #2492.

did.unittitle: "Cancer: Medical science opens new drives against the no. 2 disease"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (20:15), pages 84-86. Cancer, not cover story. Hansen database #2864.

did.unittitle: "Circulatory Systems: Blood network is cast in colored plastic"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (20:18), page 78-80. Blood vessels, two of the pages are in color on a black ground.

did.unittitle: "Bedlam 1946"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (20:18), pages 102-110, 112, 115-116, 118. "Most U.S. Mental Hospitals Are a Shamble and a Disgrace." Mentally ill, care and treatment, psychiatric hospitals, about reform of mental hospitals initiated by expose from conscientious objectors. By coincidence (?), the horror film Bedlam with Boris Karloff appeared this year. Snake Pit is not until 1948. More text than usual.

did.unittitle: "New Malaria Drugs: Sick ducks help to develop them"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:4), pages 55-57. Medicine, malaria prevention and control; photographs of injection of baby duck, of another duck, and of blood cells infected and cleared by drugs, also big chart comparing action of five drugs; vivisection.

did.unittitle: "Born this week… Life International "

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:4), page 95. Full-page advertisement announcing Life International which will appear fortnightly combining "most of two issues."

did.unittitle: "BW"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:21), pages 118-120, 122, 124, 127-128, 130. "Biological warfare, using scourges of disease and famine as weapons, is as dreadful as the atomic bomb and far more difficult to control." Chemical and biological weapons.

did.unittitle: "Tenth Anniversary Issue"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:22). Tenth anniversary issue, with good stuff on cartoons of which several are medical, as well as reprint of New Yorker parody, "an advertisement is born" (including silver nitrate drops), and a spread on the scandal of their original birth of a baby article, including photograph of police confiscating issues. Also, film article mentions popularity of mental clinics on page 138 and has still from Spellbound ." Also original baby in issue one and him now at 10.

did.unittitle: "Kidding Life "

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:22), pages 4, 6, 8, 11-12. "Cartoonists have ribbed it for a decade." Two medical cartoons.

did.unittitle: "Controversies"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:22), pages 101-102, 104. "LIFE began many, some furious, some curious, some unsettled." Group photograph of headlines.

did.unittitle: "Psychiatry"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:22), page 138. Part of article "Our Times: 1936-1946. A famous historian scans the mores and strenuous folkways of a turbulent era." About change indicated [by] new popularity of psychiatry.

did.unittitle: "Life's Baby: George Story is 10 years old"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (21:22), pages 140-141. "Where Are They Now?" Reprint from first issue and the boy's 10th birthday.

did.unittitle: "Inside the Stomach"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:6), pages 47, 49. "Science." "Tests on dog show organ at work." Fistula with camera inserted, Mt. Sinai and Wyeth hope to improve treatment of ulcers. Photographs are in color. Hansen database #2863

did.unittitle: "Disease: It is Big Army Problem"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:6), page 91. "V D Hall of Fame [Shame]," venereal disease, about soldiers in Europe being "more victims than culprits." One full-page photograph within story "Occupied Germany: Life Presents a Progress Report on the U.S. Zone." Hansen database #2863

did.unittitle: "He found the secret of making ether - and gave it away"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:6), page 97. Squibb advertisement that uses history for self-promotion. That need was for pure ether and Dr. E. R. Squibb perfected the process and "made it available to anyone who wanted it" without profit. Hansen database #2863

did.unittitle: "Human Laboratory Animals"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:8), pages 81-82, 84. "Photographs of experiments on prisoners are submitted in the trial of 23 German doctors at Nürnberg." Science, World War, 1939-1945 atrocities, Nuremberg. Hansen database #2862.

did.unittitle: "Cancer: Research Will Ultimately Find New Cures"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:16), pages 77-84, 87. Article, cancer causes, cancer prevention. Long and rich, interesting headlines and data.

did.unittitle: "Smallpox Scare"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (v. 22), pages 38-39. "New York vaccinates 2,000,000 for disease which might have spread through entire U. S." Map shows route of the man who could have been called "patient zero," as many as half million vaccinated in a day; 9 pictures, some of classic medical history scenes. New York, public health; smallpox; vaccines and vaccination. Hansen database #2861

did.unittitle: "Artificial Kidney: A vital human organ is replaced by salt solution and cellophane"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:17), pages 51-52, 54. Science, Dutch Dr. W. J. Kolff at Mt. Sinai. Hansen database #2861

did.unittitle: "Teaching the Crippled to Walk"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:18), pages 105-106, 108-111. "Rehabilitation institute in New York shows its patients how to make the most of what little they have." Science, rehabilitation; Howard A. Rusk Institute of Rehabilitation Medicine; Deaver, George Gilbert. Unclear why it's not medicine section. Story of New York Institute for the Crippled and Disabled, soon to be copied at New York University. (Rusk?)

did.unittitle: "Old Doctors: Medicine honors 400 of them who have practiced 50 years"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:25), pages 81, 83-84. Images of eight doctors (Wolf, Marks, Roe, Stage, Potter, Dowd, Stumpf, Stoddart) with short biographies, also, Dr. Whaley on another page. Continuation of article has three images (between advertising) of Dr. Whaley (1) in his office with his operating chair, (2) as a physician for the New York Central railroad (3) mixing prescriptions . The issue has a full-page advertisement for their newsreel "The March of Time," seen by 30 million minds a month, produced every four weeks by the editors of Time and Life and distributed by 20th Century-Fox. They mention four issues, including Vol. 13, No. 11, "Your Doctors: 1947. How is medical research making your life longer, safe, healthier? " Medical society of the state of New York. Hansen database #2865

did.unittitle: "'What a Babe!' Texas tomboy is first US woman to win British golf championship"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:25), pages 87-88, 90. About Babe Didrikson, tag line on last page is "Babe is a lady now, the world's most amazing athlete has learned to wear nylons and cook for her huge husband." Gender, femininity. Hansen database #2865

did.unittitle: "The March of Time"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (22:25), page 115. Advertisement "30 Million Minds a Month Focus on…" one image is an operating room. Hansen database #2865

did.unittitle: "Pain-Control Clinic"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (23:17), pages 99-102. "New York doctors ease suffering by blocking off nerves with drugs." Nerves, blocking; Pain, New York University, Bellevue. Magazine has two other stories of interest. (1) "DDT Checks Cholera in Cairo," page 46 (one aerial photograph), and (2) "Women College Presidents" with large painted portraits in color, pages 90-96. Blocking off nerves with drugs. Hansen database #2860.

did.unittitle: "What your doctor wants you to know about whooping cough vaccine"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (24:21), page 15. "The potent weapon to control the baby-killer." Rexall advertisement.

did.unittitle: "Sex Education: Oregon film provides new approach to delicate problem"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (24:21), pages 55-58, 62. Education. Is this kind of enlightened view easier before Cold War? But it's biology, not contraception.

did.unittitle: "How Smart are Animals? Even worms learn; only apes and monkeys reason"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (24:21), pages 125-131. Photographic essay.

did.unittitle: Starlet cover

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (25:12), cover. Actress Joan Diener by W. Eugene Smith. Hansen database #377

did.unittitle: "Country Doctor: His endless work has its own rewards"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (25:12), pages 115-126. Country Doctor photo essay by W. Eugene Smith. Compare to similar story that ran three months earlier on June 6th in Pittsburgh Press , Roto magazine. Also compare to public health doctor story in Saturday Evening Post of 13 May 1950. Ceriani is new in town, after a gap. Predecessor was Archer Chester Sudan, who retired after 20 years. When head of Colorado Medical Association, Sudan challenged AMA, who soon awarded him a medal. See Time January 19, 1948, pages 74 and 77. Hansen database #377

did.unittitle: "Life Goes to a Drugstore Opening"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (25:12), pages 146-148, 151-152. "Beguiled by performers, orchids and razor blades, 35,000 eager customers swarm through its doors." Los Angeles, California stores. Hansen database #377

did.unittitle: "176 Minutes Old"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:11), cover. Cover image: "Dorothy McGuire's Baby." Close-up photo of baby's face.

did.unittitle: "New Discovery about Hot Water"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:11), page 5. "Automatic water heater has a tank of glass-fused-to-steel… because glass cannot rust." Permaglass, Smithway water heater advertisement, mimics Life 's science articles.

did.unittitle: "Blue Baby Research"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:11), pages 105-109. "Anna, now 7 and pensioned, is one of the canine heroes in the long campaign that has saved hundreds of children." Medicine, blue babies, animal experiments. First surgery was in 1944 by Taussig and Blaloc.

did.unittitle: "Health by Compulsion: The President proposes much that is good, but there are better ways to achieve his goals"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:18), pages 40-41. Editorial, report by Mr. Oscar Ewing and response by AMA. "U.S. health is probably not so good as the A.M.A.'s Dr. Fishbein says it is. But it is good enough in comparison to that of the British, French," etc. Three pairs of charts each pair giving both sides. Insurance, health, public health.

did.unittitle: "Tuberculosis Vaccine: US doctors try out immunization drug after years of controversy"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:18), pages 67-68. Science. Section not noted. Tuberculosis vaccines and vaccination, lead photo is Guerin with test tubes.

did.unittitle: "Gastric Geography"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:22), pages 57-59. "Tiny camera can make photographic survey of the stomach's interior." Science. Medical photography, stomach acid and content. On a cable it can be swallowed to photograph the inside of the gastrointestinal tract; photographs show fluoroscope showing camera inside and pictures of polyps, tumors, etc. Camera is called "GastroPhotor."

did.unittitle: "Surgery in Color Television: New system will give students a doctor's-eye view of operations"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:23), pages 74-75. Medicine, television broadcasting for medical uses, only 3 photographs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (26:23), pages 106-113. Photographic essay, cortisone at Mayo; before and after; pictures of old treatments; man running without pain; compare September 9, 1949.

did.unittitle: "Hollywood's Children"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (27:3), cover.

did.unittitle: "Heterochromatic Girl"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (27:3), pages 48-49. Science. "A young Powers model displays an old genetic peculiarity." One eye brown, one blue, illustrated with huge color close-up photo of her face. Instance where they are departing from Luce's honest science, section title "Science" appears on contents page, but not on the splash page as usual.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:7), pages 46-48, 51. Medicine, poliomyelitis. Diagrams and photographs about infantile paralysis. Letters about this issue's two polio stories appeared in September 5 issue, page 4.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:10), pages 34-36, 39. Medical, betatrons, cancer therapy, radiography. It will take many more treatments to prove the value, "but whatever the outcome, the case of Fordyce Hotchkiss would make medical history." Issue also contains a few letters referring to the polio story of August 15.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:12), pages 89-90. Medicine, ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone), arthritis, cortisone. Compare cortisone article earlier on June 6, 1949. "Glands of 200,000 hogs yield only half pound of ACTH."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (27:12), pages 115-124. Photographic essay about a missionary medical effort using a boat to provide health care. Photographs by George Silk.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:12), pages 164-168. "75 years after she sold her first Vegetable Compound her descendants are still soothing female complaints." Is still in production; information on ingredients.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:12), pages 171-172. Natural history.

scopecontent.p: Life (27:18), pages 54-56. "Radioactive chemical brings about history-making recovery of patient dying from thyroid tumors." Medicine, cancer therapy, iodine isotopes. Before and after photos, plus color page of tumors!

Articles, psychiatric hospitals, has cover teaser "In this Issue: A Better Break for Mental Patients." Says it's a repsonse to their expose of five years ago (issue of May 6, 1946); includes photograph of Minnesota governor burning straitjackets.

did.unittitle: "Nurse Midwife"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Includes photograph of patients dancing in a hallway. One drug from Hoffmann-LaRoche and the other from Squibb. More powerful than Streptomycin.

did.unittitle: "Incentives Help Insane"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

did.unittitle: "Their future is at our fingertips"

scopecontent.p: Life (33:17), pages 122-123. Advertisement for Ford Motor Company, two full pages, using full page, untitled and unidentified, of Eugene Smith photograph "The Walk to Paradise Garden (1946), which was in the 1955 Steichen show "Family of Man" at the Museum of Modern Art.

did.unittitle: "The End of Polio is in Sight at Last"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Poliomyelitis, vaccines and vaccination, poliovirus, medical research, with many pictures. Photographs do not include Salk or Sabin, but do show monkeys. Jon Lindbergh on magazine cover.

did.unittitle: "Maude gets her clinic in Pineville, South Carolina"

scopecontent.p: Life (34: ), page 139-140, 143. A sequel about the money sent to Maude Callen by Life's readers, over $18,000. Photographs on 139, 140, and 143 are by W. Eugene Smith. Hansen database #3646

did.unittitle: "Charting Brain's Geography"

scopecontent.p: Life (34:17), pages 79-80. "Current applied to tiny electrodes in a monkey's head discloses functions of different cerebral areas." Science, animal experimentation, brain/localization of functions, vivisection. Experimentation with monkeys, including photographs showing monkey with wires into his brain acting differently when stimulated.

did.unittitle: "So germ-free Pasteur would have passed it!"

scopecontent.p: Life , page 151. Full-page advertisement for Dr. West's toothbrush. The same advertisement appeared in Saturday Evening Post of May 23, 1953. Three-fourths of the page is taken up by a black and white reproduction of Edelfelt's portrait of Pasteur at work, which is marked as "Copr. 1953 by Weco Products Company." Hansen database #4006

did.unittitle: "Underwater photos show fish-catching cormorant at his old and thankless job"

did.unittitle: "Science Moves in on Viruses"

scopecontent.p: Life (38:25), pages 122-124, 127-130, 133-134, 136. "Dr. Salk's polio vaccine is not an end but only the first, brilliant triumph in the all-out assault on the myriad and still mysterious spreaders of disease." Cover teaser: "The War on Virus Diseases. Robert Coughlan Tells of Salk's New Goals." LIFE's Coughlan had won Lasker award for medical journalism. Text-heavy, modest number of photographs and cartoons, touches of color in diagrams. Hansen database #2517

The film is based on the account by Robert Wallace that appeared in LIFE the prior year, May 9, 1955.

did.unittitle: Parke, Davis & Company advertisement

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (40:22), page 8. One of a series of advertisements in major magazines urging people to go to the doctor. Man in barbershop chair asks "Joe…know any sure cure for this chest pain of mine?" Barber replies "No, Sir! What's more, I've got a deal with the doctor. He doesn't cut hair, I don't practice medicine." Also page 70-76 "The Art Acquired by Yalemen."

did.unittitle: "What's Come Between You and Your Doctor?"

scopecontent.p: Life (47:15), pages 26-27. Introduction to series, "In a New Series, Life Examines a Disturbing Situation." Physicians, sick, cover story. This is introduction: "Beginning a New Series: You and Your Doctor: Science Versus Sympathy?" Image is doctor in suit, with stethoscope, at bedside, probably in a hospital, with caption "The Bedside Manner: Thing of the Past?" Part I, but not captioned as such, except for cover teaser. Also has photograph of Ehrlich. Hansen database #380

did.unittitle: "Through the Eyes of the Patient"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (47:15), pages 28-35. Surgery, photo essay. "Part I of the series." Compare New York Times of July 31, 2005 for a rerun by Art Shay "Patient's-Eye View." Hansen database #380

Physicians, could this be a turning point in moving away from pure adulation? and also in taking patient's point of view? Long text story. Hansen database #380

scopecontent.p: Life (47:16), pages 96-101, 103-104, 107-108, 113. Conclusion for "Darwin's World of Nature" series. Note it for support of evolution: "discoveries continue to affirm Darwin's great theory." Hansen database #3166

Interestingly, there's a Parke Davis Great Moment (Hippocrates) on page 152, just a few pages after end of the series. Hansen database #3166

did.unittitle: "Great Moments in Medicine"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (47:16), page 152. Parke-Davis advertisement. Runs four pages after end of Part II of "You and your doctor" that ends with Ceriani then and now. Hansen database #3166

scopecontent.p: Life (47:17), pages 142-150. "Long study and precision techniques train his hands and mind for life saving tasks." Hansen database #3167.

did.unittitle: "The Drug that Left a Trail of Heartbreak"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (53:6), pages 24-36. Cover teaser: "The Full Story of the Drug Thalidomide. The 5,000 Deformed Babies...The Woman Who Saved Thousands...The Moral Questions of Abortion and Euthanasia." Ellipses in original teaser. This is an example of the cover bullets that first appeared in 1962. Story of the Week (contrasting with something called Article of the week?). Abnormalities, thalidomide. This story has subsections with different bylines: Mulliken on Kelsey, Bunde on Drug Firm, and Oulahan on Euthanasia. Saturday Evening Post has thalidomide story several weeks later with cover teaser on October 20, 1962. Hansen database #2521

did.unittitle: "Doctors and the Rx Scandal: How Some MDs short-cut ethics and profit from their own prescriptions"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (60:25), pages 86-88, 92, 94-96, 98, 100, 102. Physicians who profit from prescribed medications, medical ethics, drugs. Cover story: "Pills like these cure illness. But some doctors seize the chance to make money from them." Title on pages 86-87: "Uneasy Balance - Ethics vs Profits. Congress weighs a bill to stop M.D.s who make money from the prescriptions they write."

did.unittitle: "A Woman on Her Way to a Miracle"

Cover photograph shows female midwife or doctor, (not in scrubs?), guiding baby's head in delivery, "Richard Falk at the age of one second." Article by Eleanor Graves, photographs by Lennart Nilsson. Cover bills Nilsson as "photographer of Life 's award-winning 'Drama of Life before Birth'," -- Might this be responding to any critics of the fetus photos?

did.unittitle: "'It's a miracle that we save any of them'"

Remodeling plans for Georgetown Hospital, Washington, D.C., hospital care; hospitals/architecture, Sick, Washington (D.C.)/hospitals; Gillespie, John Freund. Cover teaser: "Is the U.S. Hospital Fit to be Sick in? It is not designed to handle patients in crisis." Text heavy; weak pictures. Hansen database #385.

did.unittitle: "Champion of Birth Control"

did.unittitle: "Gift of a Human Heart"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (63:24), pages 24-31. Cover story: "A Dying man lives with a dead girl's heart." Color photo shows patient in bed with a man and a woman at bedside, both in scrubs. "Louis Washkansky, recipient of the historic transplant, smiles after regaining consciousness." Title on page 24A: "Gift of a Heart. Her Husband Got the Heart... His Daughter Gave It." Transplantation of organs, tissues, etc.; Darvall, Denise Ann; Washkansky, Louis. Includes a sub story about liver transplants saving little girls and serum from horses to dampen rejection. What's relevance of January 19th cover 4 weeks later? Hansen database #386.

did.unittitle: "The Corridors of the Heart"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (64:3), pages 22-31. Aorta, heart, medical photography, cardiovascular system. Cover story: "The Corridors of The Heart. Photographs by Lennart Nilsson. Views never seen before filmed inside the arteries. Camera lens set in chest cavity photographs a heart laid bare by doctors for corrective surgery." Subtitle page 22: "Extraordinary photographs of the interior of arteries show how the heart's health depends on the blood vessels that serve it." Emphasis in article is on photography with little (or no?) attention to heart transplant or artificial heart. Close-up interior photos of heart and arteries. Compare similar story of August 26, 2005. Also on page 3 there's a portrait of Nilsson by L. Wainwright. Copy 1. Hansen database #3230

did.unittitle: "The Year in Pictures 1972"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Life (73:25). This is the final weekly issue of LIFE, a "Special double issue." Only notice on cover was the small word "goodbye." For 1973-1977, five years, it was semi-annual, with one theme issue or special report and one "Year in Pictures" issue. The new monthly series started in October 1978. Then in 1991, besides the monthlies, they did four weekly issues from February 25 to March 18, because of the Gulf War.

did.unittitle: "Let's get all the evidence in this case, Judge"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Look , page 49. Full page advertisement for American Optical showing a judge having his eyes examined by "Doc" Carter, "a quarter century ago." Their point is that while old methods and facilities are now outdated, the only principle that "eye care is more than just a pair of spectacles" remains among professional men. This company is a supplier to the ophthalmic professions. Note that the image appears in a general magazine, perhaps the company is gaining loyalty by praising these practitioners and their professionalism to the wider public. Hansen database #4342

did.unittitle: "Protect Us by Thy Might"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Text begins: "very soon the National Convention of the Democratic Party will assemble in Chicago." The advertisement opposes national health insurance, but avoids saying that it is sponsored by the National Physicians Committee for the Extension of Medical Service. It notes progress on typhoid, small pox, diphtheria, tuberculosis, diabetes, and pernicious anemia. Text surrounded by red and blue ribbons. It has a coupon to clip and mail in for a free copy of booklet "Priceless Heritage." Hansen database #3123
