did.unittitle: "Daughters of Hillary: For Political Wives, She Changed the Game Forever"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York (40:35), pages 52-54 in complete issue. By Ariel Levy, with cover teaser "The Upended Gender politics of '08." Related to cover story "Bill Clinton, First Lady: How a Clinton II White House Might Work." Hansen database #4413

did.unittitle: Miscellaneous Modern Publications
"Animal Torture Worthless to Science."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: American Weekly , page 6-7, in a complete issue. Article about vivisection by Dr. Arthur V. Allen, a retired corporate physician, strongly and sentimentally anti-vivisection. Has several images, one large image is in full color. This a Hearst paper, syndicated Sunday newspaper magazine (four-color printing on newsprint), with patriotic July 4th picnic cover, 32 pages. Hansen database #4238

did.unittitle: "Dressing the Surgeon, Past and Present, for Operations."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Boston Evening Transcript , page 8. One large broadsheet page, most of which is a large illustrated article by Frederick A. Fender. An unusual subject for a daily newspaper. Four images are: photograph of top-hatted, bearded man; plague costume with large beak; caricature of group with aprons, saws, knives, etc.; and early engraving of man decorated with surgical instruments. Hansen database #3680.

did.unittitle: "Victory Against Polio! Salk's Vaccine Works."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Chicago Daily News , facsimile of front page. Banner headlines with picture of vial of vaccine above the masthead. Articles in column 3 and 7-8. Vaccine does the job; 80 to 90 percent effective medical jury tells the world. Probably removed from a book as the back of this sheet has "Space Hop A Success" from May 5, 1961. Hansen database #3391.

did.unittitle: "This picture reunited two comrades saved by Pasteur in 1885"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Child Life , page 237. Full-page advertisement, 8.5 x. 11.5 inches, from Parke Davis. With drawing of the four Newark boys on-board ship. Willy and Austin had replied to an earlier published advertisement (about the Newark boys' trip to visit Pasteur) that invited any of them to write in, and they had a reunion. One of them was also used by publicists to promote the 1935 film, "Story of Louis Pasteur." Hansen database #4402

did.unittitle: "We're fighting your enemies!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Collier's , page 55. Full-page advertisement for Budweiser discussing pasteurization process, featuring a portrait of Pasteur at the center. Hansen database #3657

did.unittitle: "The Next Great Plague to Go."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Collier's , page 5. Advertisement by Met Life with extended text about syphilis. Nicely laid out text, but no graphic images on the page at all. Hansen database #3130

did.unittitle: "Artificial heart given to man, 52."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Detroit News , page A1-A2, in complete issue. With a second article on page A2 about Schroeder receiving the second Jarvik heart in Louisville. Article notes that Schroeder lived in Michigan for many years and includes a photograph of him from 20 years earlier. Paper also has big article starting on page A1 "American HERitage" about Schlesinger library with photo of Eva Moseley. Hansen database #3274

did.unittitle: "Why some things get better all the time."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fortune . Full-page advertisement for the Union Carbide and Carbon Corporation with two scenes, "Old Pharmacy" and "Modern Drug Store," and Louis Pasteur quotation along the top: "Without laboratories men of science are soldiers without arms." Text discusses research, developments and progress of pharmaceuticals. A different advertisement with similar layout ran in 1953. Hansen database #4292

did.unittitle: "Stimulation of Labor Starting from Snuff."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Journal of the American Medical Association , pages 8-9. Two-page Parke-Davis advertisement for Pitocin. One full page shows the drawing of a fetus in utero from a work by David D. Davis, published in 1836. Hansen database #4483

did.unittitle: "The Doctor's Afraid of Doctoring."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ken , page 73. Large cartoon signed by Roir (may be Irving Roir). Tall, narrow cartoon of black ink with yellow highlights covers half the page from top to bottom; depicts the American Medical Association (AMA) as a frightened figure cowering under sheets on an operating table with the Department of Justice as the surgeon. Surgeon and assisting nurse wear masks and gloves. Ken was a left-leaning publication investigated by the House Unamerican Activities Committee. Hansen database #389

did.unittitle: Public health v. fat fees.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ken , page 23. Untitled cartoon, signed by Ellis (perhaps Fred Ellis). Tall, narrow cartoon of black ink with yellow highlights covering half the page from top to bottom, shows American Medical Association (AMA) as a figure in scrubs holding a pan balance in one hand. One side of the balance holds an exhausted figure labeled "public health" and the other holds a money bag labeled "fat fees." The doctor uses his other hand to tilt the balance toward "fat fees." Related story, "Physician, Heal Thyself," starts on page 22 (not included), ends on page 24, and appears to be written by a doctor. Ken was a left-leaning publication investigated by the House Unamerican Activities Committee. Hansen database #390

did.unittitle: "Great Moments in Medicine: Presenting the bill."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mad Magazine (1:48), back cover of complete issue. "Reproduced here, is one of a series of original oil paintings, `Practicing Medicine for Fun and Profit,' commissioned by Park-David." Satirizes the famous series of historical paintings by Robert Thom for Parke-Davis, the pharmaceutical giant, which they used in advertisements and in reproductions given to physicians for framing. This confirms the wide popular familiarity with the series. Hansen database #4244

did.unittitle: "The Microbe Hunters: Chapter IV."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Minneapolis Sunday Tribune , eight pages. Full-page feature on either page 3 or page 12 (pagination unclear), by Paul de Kruif, 4th installment, about Metchnikoff. It appears that they skipped two Pasteur chapters and one on Koch, but are running the rest, including Ehrlich. One photograph of Dr. M. and two drawings. Hansen database #4227.

did.unittitle: "No Diphtheria by 1930!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: National Geographic , single page. Metropolitan Life Insurance Company advertisement. Hansen database #2890

did.unittitle: "The Polio Panic"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Newsday , page 1, 4-5. Cover article about a 1916 outbreak of polio in the town of Oyster Bay on Long Island. Hansen database #3142

did.unittitle: "Anthrax Nation."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Daily News , pages 1-7, 24. Cover headline, "Spores found in Pataki's offices, 31 exposed at U. S. Senate, House shuts down for tests, New York and Florida strains match." Cover story "31 exposed to anthrax, House shuts down" with multiple related articles. Hansen database #3275

did.unittitle: "Life as you've never seen it."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Daily News , pages 35-37. Three-page color spread, "Famed Photog takes us inside the human experience," by Julian Kesner. These are digital photographs unlike his earlier ones. Photograph on page 35 is fetus appearing to suck a thumb. They all come from a new book, entitled Life , published by Harry N. Abrams at $45. This is evidence about the familiarity, popularity, and associated sensationalism of the Nilsson photos from fifty years earlier. Hansen database #4317

did.unittitle: "Franken-Dog: Scientists bring dead pooches back to life."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Post , pages 1-3. Upper half of front page has huge headline and close up of a beagle's face: "Scientists at a leading university have killed dozens of dogs-- then brought them back to life." Blood is drained for up to three hours. Patrick Kochanek, director of Safar Center for Resuscitation Research at the University of Pittsburgh. Article also quotes PETA representative. Similar story appeared in New York Times Magazine of December 11, 2005. Hansen database #4159

did.unittitle: "Dr. Germ: Saddam's scientists behind anthrax outbreak."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Post , pages 1-6. Cover story. Hansen database #3296

did.unittitle: "Glory, Set, Go."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Press (11:21), pages 36-37. Two-page review by John Strausbaugh of an exhibit of graphic materials on President Garfield's assassin Charles Guiteau and his murder trial with insanity defense. Exhibit was at the library of the New York Academy of Medicine and based largely on cartoons and caricatures from the Hansen Collection. Hansen database #4505

did.unittitle: "What's Up, Doc?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The New York Sun , page 19, in complete issue. Lengthy review by Ivan Oransky, MD, with two large color illustrations, of an exhibit of some comic books on medical doctors and scientists from the Bert Hansen Collection, in the library of the New York Academy of Medicine (NYAM). Hansen database #4496.

did.unittitle: "Pasteur Rids the World of Hydrophobia: In Cords It's the 'Know How' that Counts."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times Sunday Magazine , page 6. Full page advertisement for Raycrest Fabrics with picture of Pasteur watching Jupille injection. The theme is, "When science couldn't find answers, Pasteur could, because he had 'know how.' And we have know how in our mills for making cords that are lightweight for summer wear." They are manufacturers of rayons and fine cottons, at a New York City address; Nathan Sheinman, president. Hansen database #3535

did.unittitle: "Health Breakthroughs: New York Area Hospitals Prepare for the 21st Century."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times Magazine , Special Advertising Supplement. Cover is color photograph close-up of man heavily garbed in sterile scrubs with helmet and mask, holding a piece of shiny chrome metal (perhaps a hip joint?) in a gloved hand. Similar item from 2001 is #3276. Hansen database #3223

did.unittitle: "Health Breakthroughs: New York Area Hospitals Continue to Lead the World."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , Special Advertising Supplement. Note persistence of notion of latest discovery as the primary sell, novelty instead of caring staff or reliability. Newest rather than best or conflating them to assume newest is best. Hansen database #3152

did.unittitle: "Genetic Code of Human Life is Cracked by Scientists."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , page A1, A21-24. Banner headline on page one with related articles by Natalie Angier, "A Pearl and a Hodgepodge: Human DNA," and Nicholas Wade, "A Shared Success: 2 Rivals' Announcement Marks New Medical Era, Risks and All." Includes diagrams of human DNA "the book of life" and photograph of Collins and Venter. Includes advertisement on A5 for Compaq computers referencing the discovery of DNA and op-ed piece on page A23 "Where the Genome Leads Us." Hansen database #3151.

did.unittitle: "Med Update."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , advertising supplement. 16-page magazine on smooth, but non-glossy paper. Compare to #3223 of October 6, 1996, which was called "Health Breakthroughs." This one is graphically calmer, and might have a different emphasis. Useful record of current imagery of the profession. Hansen database #3276

did.unittitle: "A Revolution at 50: DNA Changed the World. Now What?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , pages F1-F12. Tuesday Science Section, multiple articles. Hansen database #3931

did.unittitle: "Diagnosis Goes Low Tech."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , page B9. Article featuring a quarter-page reprint of Eugene Smith photograph of country doctor carrying his bag as he walks in tall grass. Example of the continued visibility and familiarity of this series of photographs more than 50 years after they were published in Life . Hansen database #4486

did.unittitle: "Medicine's Lost Art."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , advertising supplement, page 1. Promoting subscriptions to the Large Type Weekly edition, using a Eugene Smith photo as the main image at center of this page. Uses art and headlines from the article in Hansen database #4486. Evidence for continuing popularity and familiarity of Smith series from more than 50 years earlier. Hansen database #4487

did.unittitle: "Even if animal research resulted in a cure for AIDS, we'd be against it."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times Sunday Magazine , verso of back cover. Very unusual full-page advertisement against PETA and for AIDS research. Shows a hand putting a match to an AIDS ribbon with a quote from the activist Ingrid Newkirk "Even if animal research resulted in a cure for AIDS, we'd be against it." Published by Consumer Freedom.com. Hansen database #4037

did.unittitle: "Patient's-Eye View: A Photojournalist Covers His Own Surgery."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times Sunday Magazine , page 54. With photographs and captions by Art Shay, in section called "Lives," open to unsolicited manuscripts. This might be the last page except for inside cover, usually given over to essays by outsiders. Compare to Life, October 12, 1959, page 28-35: "Through the eyes of the patient" by McCombe. Hansen database #4171

did.unittitle: "When a Doctor Is More, and Less, Than a Healer"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: New York Times , pages D1-D8. Four clippings of Abigail Zuger's review of "Picturing Medical Progress," in Science Times section, illustration of Walter Reed in black and white. Copy delivered in Binghamton, NY, (#4437) has the image in color. Hansen database #4492

did.unittitle: "A Hospital at Night."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On Call , cover of complete issue. "A Community-Wide Newspaper, Issued by South Nassau Communities Hospital." 24 pages on newsprint. An informal local publication, not kept by libraries, but useful for recording contemporary images of doctors and nurses at work. Cover photograph shows doctor and nurses putting a case on a boy's arm for a shoulder fracture. More photographs on the center fold, mostly of nurses. All photographs are black and white. Hansen database #3910

did.unittitle: "Stamp out Typhoid!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pictorial Review , page 33. Full-page advertisement by Metropolitan Life Insurance Company depicts women and boys outdoors, possibly camping, drinking from a stream. Hansen database #68

did.unittitle: "Country Doctor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Pittsburgh Press , Sunday ROTO Magazine, cover of complete issue. Photo essay as cover story. Compare to W. Eugene Smith story that ran in Life on September 20, 1948, just 3 months later. Subject is Edgar H. MacKinlay practicing in McConnellsburg and Fulton County. Photographs are by Steward Love. MacKinlay finished at Columbia Medical School about 15 years ago. Came here to fill in for the aged and ailing town physician John W. Mosser, before returning to NYC for a residency in EENT but instead stayed here for good. Hansen database #3810

did.unittitle: "Infantile Paralysis."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Real Romances (6:7), page 14-15, 61-62, in complete issue. By Alan Hynd, under section title "Health Clinic." Important polio documentation in a woman's magazine with very early mention of a possible vaccine. Many kinds of information with several photographs of victims and helpers. One subtitle is "Is There a Polio Vaccine?" and it mentions March of Dimes researchers having produced polio antibodies in the blood of monkeys by injecting them with live virus. Also mentions Sister Kenny. Hansen database #3806

did.unittitle: "Strange Medical Facts."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Strange Medical Facts (1:4), complete issue. Published bimonthly. Cover photo has a couple about to kiss. Despite the sensationalistic presentation, this is not fiction like that found in National Enquirer, but basically OK science. Among the articles of interest are Leeches (pages 20-21, 56); monkey gland work of Voronoff and Steinach (pages 26-27, 51-52); use of curare in dealing with paralysis from polio (pages 36-37, 58, 62); and pre-frontal lobotomy (pages 44-45, 70). This might be the same publication noted (in #4094) for some date in 1954 called True Strange Medical Facts. Hansen database #4191

did.unittitle: Diphtheria posters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Westvaco Inspirations for Printers (v. 62), page 1228. Full page depicts four prize winning posters designed by art students in 1930 contest. Winners were selected from submissions from eighty schools throughout New York State. This trade magazine was published in the 1930s. Main phrases include "Protect Me" "Toxin-Antitoxin A Life Saver," 'Parents... Toxin antitoxin Prevents Diphtheria." Hansen database #3241

did.unittitle: "For us, he fought an endless battle with disease."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman's Home Companion (56:4), page 52. Full page Squibb advertisement with watercolor of Hideyo Noguchi with Africans. Unpaginated sheet from Woman's Home Companion in 1929. Note: Copyright 1929 by E. R. Squibb is on the page. Hansen database #4316

did.unittitle: "Their Spirit Lives on."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman's Home Companion , page 3. Full-page magazine advertisement for Listerine antiseptic honoring Pasteur, Lister, and Leeuwenhoek, with portraits floating in a cloud above a man with microscope and lab equipment. Text opens "Pasteur, reviewing the bacteria of disease, Lister making surgery antiseptic while great men jeered, Van Leeuwenhoek, peering through his crude lenses, the first to actually see germs-all were men of relentless curiosity, fierce enthusiasm and tireless application." Other names in a banner "their spirit lives on" are Behring, Jenner, Rollins, Metchnikoff, Roux, and Reed. Hansen database #4469

did.unittitle: "…A Fortune for Playthings."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman's Home Companion , page 27. Full-page magazine advertisement for Listerine tooth paste. Shows a very rich woman with a yacht, jewelry, etc. who still uses the 25-cent tooth paste because of its results. Hansen database #4470

did.unittitle: "Why Risk Typhoid?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: World's Work , page 83. Full page Metropolitan Life Insurance Company advertisement. "20 times more dangerous than lightning!" Hansen database #2891

did.unittitle: "Louis Pasteur" and "Biographies in Brief."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journals. Petrolagar advertisements. The first is a caricature by "T" of Louis Pasteur holding a rabbit, originally in Vanity Fair. The second advertisement includes "authoritative sketches...compiled by Walter R. Bett, former librarian of the College of Physicians and Surgeons at Columbia University." Includes information about Thomas Henry Huxley, Charles Darwin, and Oliver Weldell Holmes. Hansen database #3127

did.unittitle: "M. and Madam Curie."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Petrolagar advertisement with caricature by Imp, originally in Vanity Fair. Text indicates that one can write them to request copies of the prints. Hansen database #4481

did.unittitle: "Professor Rudolph Virchow."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Petrolagar advertisement caricature by Spy, originally in Vanity Fair. Hansen database #4482

did.unittitle: "Sir William Crookes."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Petrolagar advertisement with caricature by Imp, originally in Vanity Fair. Hansen database #4480

did.unittitle: "Pavlov (Ivan Petrovich Pavlov) 1849-1936."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Wyeth advertisement with Pavlov image, number three in a series of Wyeth advertisements that uses biography and portraits of Pavlov, Roentgen, Sippy, and others. Might be earlier than 1970s as the artist Ernest Hamlin Baker (1889-1975) was very active in the 1930s and 1940s; he did hundreds of Time cover portraits. These portraits are exceptionally attractive work and more artistic than the historical illustrations done in some other advertisements. Hansen database #3126

did.unittitle: "William Conrad Roentgen, 1845-1923."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Number four in a series of Wyeth advertisements, biography and portraits of Pavlov, Roentgen, and Sippy. Might be earlier than 1970s as the artist Ernest Hamlin Baker (1889-1975) was very active in the 1930s and 1940s; he did hundreds of Time cover portraits. These portraits are exceptionally attractive work and more artistic than the historical illustrations done in some other advertisements. Hansen database #4461

did.unittitle: "Bertram Welton Sippy, 1866-1924."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified medical journal. Image in a series of Wyeth advertisements, biography and portraits of Pavlov, Roentgen, and Sippy. Might be earlier than 1970s as the artist Ernest Hamlin Baker (1889-1975) was very active in the 1930s and 1940s; he did hundreds of Time cover portraits. These portraits are exceptionally attractive work and more artistic than the historical illustrations done in some other advertisements. Hansen database #4462

did.unittitle: "Protect Babies Before Birth."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unidentified publication. Full page anti-syphilis advertisement from Metropolitan Life Insurance Company. Protect babies by testing mothers. Looks like National Geographic with a full color Matson cruise line advertisement on the reverse. Hansen database #2893

did.unittitle: Ephemera

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series are arranged by date and item title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains items with medical themes or imagery produced by various agencies, corporations, and organizations. Items include medical history themed prints, publicity materials for Hollywood films about physicians, brochures for medical devices, health department signs, calendars, and posters.

did.unittitle: "Thomas A. Edison - The Wizard Of Menlo Park."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Selmar Hess "Great Men and Famous Women" portfolio, part #50 of 68 parts. Image originally appeared as Frank Leslie's Illustrated Newspaper front cover January 10, 1891, (Hansen #933). Print is credited as "copyright by Frank Leslie's Weekly." A similar image appeared on the quack medicine booklet "Weak and Debilitated Men" (#3088). Hansen database #3091

did.unittitle: "Anti-diphtheritic serum instructions."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two sheets of instructions for preparing and administering injection of a Parke-Davis anti-diphtheritic serum, removed from a shipping box for the vial so they could be flattened out. Includes list of other Parke-Davis bacteriological products like anti-streptococic serum, anti-tetanic (lockjaw) serum, and anti-tubercic serum. Also tuberculin for detecting tuberculosis in cattle. Hansen database #4029

did.unittitle: New York Pharmacal Association calendar.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advertising calendar with images of Virchow, McDowell, Pasteur, Sims, Laennec, Malpighi, Harvey, Jenner, Loomis, Cross, Sydenhan, and Bright. Odd selection of heroes seems to result from matching them to their birth months. Portraits have birth and death dates, but no first names or note of achievement. "Compliments of Lactopeptine, New York Pharmacal Association, Yonkers." Hansen database #3147

did.unittitle: "Grotus Maris: A Boon for Men."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advertising folder for "Grotus Maris (Strength of the Male)" apparatus to help impotent men keep an erection. "A Scientific Development for Your Men Patients." Designed by a physician who specialized in Genito-Urinary Diseases. Manufactured by Illinois Surgical Supply Co. 10 South Wells Street, Chicago, IL. Lots of advertisement copy and information on cause of impotency. Large illustration of the rubber device. Don't know if this was a legitimate or a quack medical device. Says it was sold only to doctors and pharmacists. 3 1/8 x 5 1/2" folded, opens to a 6 1/4 x 11" sheet. When folded, this is the same size and style as the street guides that peddled information about venereal disease. Perhaps it was even an insert in such guides. Hansen database #3380

did.unittitle: "Health Campaign: For the Protection of Life and Health."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure by Met Life. Advice addresses flies, mosquitoes, milk, soap, nourishment, and tuberculosis. A four-fold pocket size booklet with cartoon-like drawings. Includes image of knight fighting the dragon of tuberculosis. Two-color printing, only yellow and black. Size when folded is 3.5 x 5.5 inches. Hansen database #3146

did.unittitle: "Cholene."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advertising brochure. Pasteur's Stop-a-Pain Tablets are one item included on this small tri-fold brochure from the Myers Chemical Company in Leon, Iowa. Main product here is Cholene, a "jelly or ointment" in "collapsible tubes" that destroys your invisible enemies, stops pain and inflammation, fights Catarrh, a cold in the head, croup, piles and other great stuff! The back page has an advertisement for Pasteur's Stop-A-Pain Tablets and Myers' Anti-Constipation Tablets. When folded, the size is 3 x 6 inches. Printed in black ink on cheap paper. Hansen database #3406

did.unittitle: "Feel Like a Million."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sheet music promoting Nature's Remedy Tablets, words and music by Will Waldron. Cover image is man singing "It's in the Air" into a radio microphone. Behind him there are bags of money and there are stacks of coins and bills on the table holding the microphone. Product is a "natural laxative." Hansen database #3140

did.unittitle: "Chelsea Hospital, London -- 1810."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: #5 in a set of 6 hospital prints produced by Petrolagar Medicine Laboratories as an advertising premium. Each print has a label with the title and "reproduced from the original Aquatints drawn and engraved by Rowlandson & Pugin." Labels are gummed and meant to be applied to the back of the print after they were framed. "Courtesy Petrolagar Laboratories, Inc., Chicago" is printed in the lower right-hand corner of each print. These are definitely well-done reproductions of the original early 1800s prints. Hansen database #4491

did.unittitle: "College of Physicians, London -- 1808."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: #4 in a set of 6 hospital prints produced by Petrolagar Medicine Laboratories as an advertising premium. Each print has a label with the title and "reproduced from the original Aquatints drawn and engraved by Rowlandson & Pugin." Labels are gummed and meant to be applied to the back of the print after they were framed. "Courtesy Petrolagar Laboratories, Inc., Chicago" is printed in the lower right-hand corner of each print. These are definitely well-done reproductions of the original early 1800s prints. Hansen database #4490

did.unittitle: "Middlesex Hospital, London 1808."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: #1 in a set of 6 hospital prints produced by Petrolagar Medicine Laboratories as an advertising premium. Each print has a label with the title and "reproduced from the original Aquatints drawn and engraved by Rowlandson & Pugin." Labels are gummed and meant to be applied to the back of the print after they were framed. "Courtesy Petrolagar Laboratories, Inc., Chicago" is printed in the lower right-hand corner of each print. These are definitely well-done reproductions of the original early 1800s prints. Hansen database #1370

did.unittitle: "St. Lukes's Hospital, London -- 1809."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: #2 in a set of 6 hospital prints produced by Petrolagar Medicine Laboratories as an advertising premium. Each print has a label with the title and "reproduced from the original Aquatints drawn and engraved by Rowlandson & Pugin." Labels are gummed and meant to be applied to the back of the print after they were framed. "Courtesy Petrolagar Laboratories, Inc., Chicago" is printed in the lower right-hand corner of each print. These are definitely well-done reproductions of the original early 1800s prints. Hansen database #4488

did.unittitle: "Surrey Institution, London -- 1809."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: #3 in a set of 6 hospital prints produced by Petrolagar Medicine Laboratories as an advertising premium. Each print has a label with the title and "reproduced from the original Aquatints drawn and engraved by Rowlandson & Pugin." Labels are gummed and meant to be applied to the back of the print after they were framed. "Courtesy Petrolagar Laboratories, Inc., Chicago" is printed in the lower right-hand corner of each print. These are definitely well-done reproductions of the original early 1800s prints. Hansen database #4489

did.unittitle: "Coca Cola Famous Doctors Series: William Harvey."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Coca Cola Educational Material. One out of a set of 6 heavy paper folders, 12" x 17" open. Hansen database #4473

did.unittitle: "Coca Cola Famous Doctors Series: Hippocrates."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Coca Cola Educational Material. Text includes the Hippocratic oath. One out of a set of 6 heavy paper folders, 12" x 17" open. Hansen database #4472

did.unittitle: "Coca Cola Famous Doctors Series: Joseph Lister."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Coca Cola Educational Material. One out of a set of 6 heavy paper folders, 12" x 17" open. Hansen database #4474

did.unittitle: "Coca Cola Famous Doctors Series: Louis Pasteur."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Coca Cola Educational Material. One out of a set of 6 heavy paper folders, 12" x 17" open. Hansen database #436

did.unittitle: "Your Doctor and You."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Booklet by Parke, Davis & Company, 11" x 14," 16 pages. "Recent advertisements in a series which has been appearing in leading magazines since 1928." Each page depicts one advertisement. Topics include appendicitis, cancer, heart disease, and others. Many of the narratives in these pages are about parental mistakes to be avoided by seeing one's physician. The audience for this expensive book is not clear, may have been physicians (series emphasized seeing and trusting your doctor), management, or others with corporate interests. Hansen database #3513

did.unittitle: "The Story of Louis Pasteur."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Movie herald. Visually striking, two-color printing on both sides, roughly 9 x 11.5 inches. One side is portrait style movie poster, with a more than usual sensationalized text. Other side is half in Spanish. Provenance unknown, but the margin includes "Continental Litho Corp Cleveland Ohio Made in U.S.A." According to an internet source, "Movie Heralds were placed in pressbooks (a booklet introducing the movie to the press, magazines , newspapers, etc.), and they used images similar and at times different from the movie posters or lobby cards." Hansen database #4438

did.unittitle: "Paul Muni in 'The Story of Louis Pasteur'."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Original glossy advertisement slick for 1936 Warner Bros. film "The Story Of Louis Pasteur" starring Paul Muni, Josephine Hutchinson, Donald Woods, and Anita Louise. Advertising slicks provide graphical material for film advertisements in various sizes and layouts so that a local paper can use these images and titles with local times, dates, and theater name added in the white space. Hansen database #3828

did.unittitle: "The Birth of a Baby: Before Your Very Eyes."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Six color lobby cards for the movie, "Presented by the American Commmittee for Maternal Welfare, Inc." 11 x 14, a little garish in appearance because of strength of the yellow color (did other colors fade more to create this effect or did the cards originally look like this?). Compare to Life 's story in 1938. Hansen database #3843

did.unittitle: Louis Pasteur's Will

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Facsimile of Louis Pasteur's handwritten Last will and Testament March 29, 1877 ; copyrighted 1939 by Vichy Celestins mineral water company; possibly linked to the New York City World's Fair. The document is in French with English translation: "This is my last testament. I leave to my wife, everything the law allows me to leave her. May my children never stray from the path of righteousness, and keep forever for their mother the tenderness she deserves. L. Pasteur." Hansen database #4360

did.unittitle: "Search and Research, A Pictorial Portfolio of Scenes and Characters in the Drama of Medicine and Medical Progress."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Spiral bound art portfolio from Abbott Laboratories. 15" x 12", with eight photographs (14" x 11") by Sarra and four drawings (10.5" x 7") by Pierre Brissaud. Hansen database #4177

did.unittitle: "Fight for Life."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Original movie house poster on heavy card stock for use in display case outside the theater. Tall, narrow film poster (14 x 36 in.), light purple background with red dress and yellows too, for Pare Lorentz film. Hansen database #4410

did.unittitle: "Cibalgine, 'Ciba'"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Tri-fold promotional brochure for Cibalgine, an "antiseptic, antipyretic, sedative," from Ciba Pharmaceutical Products, Inc. Cover shows a watercolor painting of St. Vincent's Hospital in Erie, Pennsylvania, a three-story brick building with frame porch and mansard roof, text describes the opening in 1875 and notes that this is "From a Series of Interesting Events in Medical History." Hansen database #4484

did.unittitle: "Eulogy of the Doctor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hayley M-O Company gift print of work by Robert Louis Stevenson. Designed to imitate a Renaissance book's illuminated page with border, rubrics, etc., but image at bottom right is of a microscope, and image at bottom center is of an office with a telephone on the desk. Text begins "There are men and classes of men that stand above the common herd.... rarer still the clergyman, the physician almost as a rule. He is the flower of our civilization ...." Much about manners and good cheer and keeping secrets, but nothing on science or progress in his eulogy. "Compliments of The Haley M-O Company, Inc." printed in modern type at the bottom. The company was a producer of mineral oil. Hansen database #4478

did.unittitle: "The Wheel O' Life."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Correct eating health chart" volvelle by Dr. Adolphus Hohensee, "author, lecturer, food authority." Device is printed in red and black on heavy card stock and consists of two circular paper boards fastened in the center so they rotate independently. Central area of top wheel is divided into segments, similar to a lottery wheel, that contain descriptions of minerals and vitamins, outer edge of back wheel shows names of various foods. As the bottom wheels are rotated small holes in the top wheel display the amount of vitamins, minerals, and calories for each food. Minerals include sodium, potassium, magnesium, iron, chlorine, phosphorus, calcium, sulphur, silicon, fluorine, iodine, and manganese. Hansen database #3141

did.unittitle: "The History of Anesthesia Study Chart No. 1: Pain…A Heritage and a Challenge."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Promotional poster for the film "The Great Moment" directed by Preston Sturgis, starring Joel McCrae and Betty Field, about W. T. G. Morton, a Boston dentist who promoted the use of ether as a surgical anesthetic. Poster features multiple images with text depicting moments in medical history related to alleviation of pain during medical procedures, including "Mesmer's magic tub."

did.unittitle: "The History of Anesthesia Study Chart No. 2: They Fought the Fight."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Promotional poster for the film "The Great Moment" directed by Preston Sturgis, starring Joel McCrae and Betty Field, about W. T. G. Morton, a Boston dentist who promoted the use of ether as a surgical anesthetic. Poster features multiple images with text depicting moments in medical history related to alleviation of pain during medical procedures. "Men of Science" in center of poster includes Joseph Priestly, Antoine Laurent Lavoisier, Michael Farraday, and Willhelm Serturner.

did.unittitle: "The History of Anesthesia Study Chart No. 3: Benefactor of Mankind."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Promotional poster for the film "The Great Moment" directed by Preston Sturgis, starring Joel McCrae and Betty Field, about W. T. G. Morton, a Boston dentist who promoted the use of ether as a surgical anesthetic. Poster features multiple images with text depicting moments in medical history related to alleviation of pain during medical procedures. Discusses use of nitrous oxide and development of hollow needle and hypodermic syringe.

did.unittitle: "The History of Anesthesia Study Chart No. 4: Pain Where is Thy Sting?"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Promotional poster for the film "The Great Moment" directed by Preston Sturgis, starring Joel McCrae and Betty Field, about W. T. G. Morton, a Boston dentist who promoted the use of ether as a surgical anesthetic. Poster features multiple images with text depicting moments in medical history related to alleviation of pain during medical procedures. Includes images of WW I operating rooms and photographs of "modern" dental office and operating room.

did.unittitle: "The Seven Ages of a Physician."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Large format gift book (12 x 15 inch) from CIBA Pharmaceutical portfolio with plastic ring binding. Paintings are by James Chapin, they might also have been used in advertising. Titles: (1) The Doctor's Son (mother with baby), (2) The Dying Hare-A Boys Dedication to Healing (hunting), (3) Medical Education-The Bedside Lecture, (4) The Doctor (holding newborn, standing with boy on crutches, and old lady sitting down), (5) The Doctor In War (receiving hypo from a nurse to inject a dirty-faced wounded soldier, with crowd of wounded standing like a chorus behind), (6) Research Heroic-The Self Inoculation (glassware in his lab is glittering and glowing, it's almost psychedelic), and (6) Doctor's Heritage (old doc and young one holding test-tube with book, globe, and skull on the desk). Final onion-skin page at the end has two "Suggestions for framing." Hansen database #1649

did.unittitle: Armour Laboratories physicians of color portfolio.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Set of eleven prints in a gift portfolio for physicians of color by Armour Laboratories. Paintings by F. Netter, M.D., very famous as a medical illustrator showing body parts, endocrine system, etc. Scenes of people in medical settings like these are not well known as part of his work. Portfolio is captioned: "Compliments of Armour Laboratories." Titles: ambulance call, anatomy lab, catastrophe, class reunion, clinic, country night call, emergency ward, gyn examining room, osteology cram, pediatric ward-convalescent, and reconversion. Includes part of original mailing envelope postmarked May 20, 1948, in Chicago; sent to doctor in Ohio. Armour Laboratories products included sutures and chemical preparations. It's possible that this work is just prior to Netter's long association with Ciba. One print of set is missing. Hansen database #3524

did.unittitle: "Know your Doctor."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Publication by the R. J. Reynolds Tobacco Company with thirteen glossy full-color pictures suitable for framing. Hard cover with plastic spiral binding, 15.5" x 12.75." Artwork is from a series of medical history themed advertisements in national magazines for Camel cigarettes. Accompanying letter states that the book was put together and sent to doctors because of the mounting requests for frameable copies of the advertisements. Two pictures employ medical history images: "Lady with a Lamp" is a female doctor with head reflector with text mentioning Nightingale, Blackwell, Jacobi, Jane Viola Meyers, and Anna Broomall; and "Big Game Hunters" shows two doctors working with a microscope in a jungle hut. Hansen database #3514

did.unittitle: Iowa smallpox isolation sign.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sign has signature of official but no house address. Quarantine signs that were actually used (with signature or street address) are hard to find as they tended to be discarded. Unused blank signs from administrative offices are more common. Copy 1. Hansen database #3129

did.unittitle: Iowa smallpox isolation sign.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sign has pencil signature of official and a house address of 1408 West State St. Quarantine signs that were actually used (with signature or street address) are hard to find as they tended to be discarded. Unused blank signs from administrative offices are more common. Copy 2. Hansen database #3813

did.unittitle: "Conquerors of Yellow Fever."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Wyeth medical history print, depicts Walter Reed and others. Caption under bottom left of image: "Wyeth, Philadelphia;" under bottom right: "by Dean Cornwell, N.A." Hansen database #4494.

did.unittitle: "The Father of American Pharmacy: William Proctor Jr., 1817-1872."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Wyeth medical history print, depicts Proctor and an assistant with laboratory equipment. Caption under bottom left of image: "Wyeth, Philadelphia;" under bottom right: "by Dean Cornwell, N.A." Hansen database #2340.

did.unittitle: "Monastic Pharmacy (5th to 12th Centuries)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Print from Parke, Davis & Company "A History of Pharmacy in Pictures" series. From a painting by Robert Thom, based on garden at the Cloisters. Hansen database #4495.

did.unittitle: "The Era of Biologicals."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Print from Parke, Davis & Company "A History of Pharmacy in Pictures" series. From a painting by Robert Thom, shows horses being used to create diphtheria antitoxin. Hansen database #2341.

did.unittitle: "A History of Medicine in Pictures," part 1.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Set of five prints by Parke, Davis & Company includes Lind, Jenner, Harvey, Vesalius, and Rhazes. Each labeled "One of a series: A History of Medicine in Pictures, presented by Parke, Davis & Company." Hansen database #4475

did.unittitle: "A History of Medicine in Pictures," part 2.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Set of five prints by Parke, Davis & Company includes Medieval Hospitals, Paré, Paracelsus, Pinel, and Galen. Each labeled "One of a series: A History of Medicine In Pictures presented by Parke, Davis & Company." Hansen database #4476

did.unittitle: "A History of Medicine in Pictures" descriptive texts and framing instructions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Parke, Davis and Company "A History of Medicine in Pictures" series of prints. Texts "may be clipped and attached to backs of framed pictures." Framing instructions include photographs of doctor's office, waiting room, and examining room showing images on the wall. Two copies of framing instructions plus several editions of texts. Hansen database #1331

did.unittitle: "Benjamin Rush: Physician, Pedant, Patriot."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Print from Parke, Davis and Company "A History of Medicine in Pictures" series. From of Robert Thom painting, with sheet of related text. Labeled "One of a series: A History of Medicine in Pictures, presented by Parke, Davis & Company." Text has been cut from a larger sheet of multiple texts (#1331). Hansen database #4479

did.unittitle: "Pasteur and the Germ Theory: Jackdaw Number S3"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Compiled by J. K. Crellin, edited by Gerald Leach, Grossman Publishers, New York City. Jackdaw Publications science instruction/exhibition packet. Hansen database #4048

did.unittitle: "Faraday and Electricity: Jackdaw Number S5"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Compiled by Colin Ronan, edited by Gerald Leach, Grossman Publishers, New York City. Jackdaw Publications science instruction/exhibition packet. Hansen database #4049

did.unittitle: "Bellevue Hospital: 250 Years in Service to New York."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster for a 1986-87 exhibit at the Museum of the City of New York. A quality enlargement of an 1896 photograph with a surgeon stepping into a horse-drawn ambulance outside the Bellevue building. Further historical images and text on the back. 16 x 27 inches. Hansen database #3143

did.unittitle: "Oh Doctor: Images of the health professions in the popular arts."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster for Helfand exhibit at National Library of Medicine with symposium on October 2, 1987. Image is 1920 song sheet "Oh Doctor." Hansen database #2899

did.unittitle: "The Truth: AIDS Is Germ Warfare...Against Gays and Blacks!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: United Front Against Racism and Capitalism-Imperialism newsprint broadside, version 2. Posted around NYC by the radical group. 17 x 23 inches. Version 1 (#2342) does not mention blacks and gays in the headline. Hansen database #3113.

did.unittitle: "The Truth: AIDS Is Germ Warfare."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: United Front Against Racism and Capitalism-Imperialism newsprint broadside, version 1. Posted around New York City by the radical group . 17 x 23 inches. Version 2 (#3113) mention blacks and gays in the headline. Hansen database #2342.

did.unittitle: "Les cent ans de l'Institut Pasteur: Colloque International sur l'Histoire de l'Institut Pasteur, 6-10 Juin 1988."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pasteur Institute centenary celebration poster, printed in red and blue ink on glossy white heavy paper, 32 x 24 inches. Hansen database #3133

did.unittitle: "Surgery Demonstration at Massachusetts General Hospital."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Page from "The History of Medicine 1991 Calendar" from the National Library of Medicine. Reprint of 1889 hand-tinted wood engraving, 9 x 12 inches. Hansen database #3145

did.unittitle: "Positive Reflections on Art."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: AIDS themed monthly calendar, spiral bound. From the Abbott Laboratories Inc. "Positively Art" campaign, with assistance from Visual AIDS, an organization promoting AIDS awareness. Includes two pieces by Joe De Hoyos. Hansen database #3112

did.unittitle: "They Shall Serve Mankind Forever."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Modern reproduction of 1935 double-page advertisement for E. R. Squibb featuring medical heroes. By F. R. Gruger, glossy paper, 11x17 inches. Depicts Welch, Nightingale, Long, Morton, Lister, Koch, Pasteur, Ehrlich, Semmelweis, Jenner, and Bernard. Image appeared in several magazines including Collier's, Time, and Saturday Evening Post. One example is a two-page spread in Collier's Magazine, January 19, 1935 on unnumbered pages following page 29. Hansen database #3128

did.unittitle: "An Infectious Idea: 125 Years of Public Health in Toronto."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Miscellaneous pictorial materials connected with the celebration of 125 Years of Public Health in Toronto, Ontario, Canada at the City of Toronto Archives. Six bookmarks, two posters. Hansen database #4464

did.unittitle: Reference copies

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains photocopies of primary sources and microfilm created by Bert Hansen while conducting research on the depiction of medicine and public health in popular graphic art. The bulk of the photocopies depict material not included in the collection and which is not readily available online.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Contents of the series were removed from binders and rehoused in archival folders for preservation. Copies are arranged according to Hansen's original order, with dates reflecting dates of original materials
